target.title: feat(agents): opt-in self-evolving SOUL.md via reflection sub-turn + soul_update
target.body:
## Summary

Closes #95790.

Adds opt-in `SOUL.md` auto-evolution: signal-keyword user messages trigger a dedicated reflection sub-turn that lets the model decide whether to persist a rule via a new `soul_update` tool. Forced notice + `openclaw soul undo` per the issue.

> **AI-assisted PR.** Designed and drafted with Claude/Codex assistance. I understand what every piece of this code does — happy to walk through any section in review.

## What Problem This Solves

The `SOUL.md` template tells the agent the file is "yours to evolve. As you learn who you are, update it" — but the runtime never makes it evolve. There is no code path that appends to `SOUL.md` during a conversation. Users restate the same preferences ("no em-dashes", "be more terse", "no emojis") every session and the personality layer never matures.

This PR closes the docs-vs-runtime gap by adding an opt-in evolution mechanism that:

- Is **off by default** (`agents.defaults.soul.autoUpdate: false`) so no behavior changes for existing users.
- Is **gated to a single context** — `soul_update` is only registered for the model inside the dedicated reflection sub-turn, never in main turns (see gating test).
- Has **forced user notice** (`Added to SOUL.md: '<rule>'`) that cannot be suppressed when a rule lands.
- Is **reversible** — `openclaw soul undo` removes the last auto-added entry.
- Is **injection-hardened** — HTML-comment delimiters in rule/evidence are neutralized before any write to SOUL.md so a hostile value cannot escape the evidence comment and persist arbitrary instructions into the bootstrap file (closes clawsweeper P1 finding).

### What's in
- New: `soul-reflection.ts`, `soul-auto-update.ts`, `soul-reflection-runner.ts`, `tools/soul-update-tool.ts`, `cli/soul-cli.ts`
- Schema knob `agents.defaults.soul.autoUpdate` (Zod + TS) plus matching help text + UI label so the new key shows up in config discovery, default false
- Ingress hookup in `agentCommandInternal` — calls `maybeFireSoulReflection` before main turn; sub-turn runs internal with no recursion
- `soul_update` gated: only registers when `enableSoulUpdateTool === true` AND `autoUpdate === true`; flag is only true inside the sub-turn (via `inputProvenance.sourceTool === "soul_reflection"`)
- `soul_update` tool schema: `rule` is **optional** at the provider boundary so the noop path passes provider-side validation; runtime still enforces "rule OR noop=true"
- Tool catalog entry under `agents`, all profiles
- `docs/concepts/soul.md` updated

## Evidence

**Real environment tested.** Local OpenClaw build on this branch (head `2ba442c`), workspace at `~/openclaw-soulsmoke-ws/`, `agents.defaults.soul.autoUpdate: true`, default model claude-haiku-4-5, Windows 11 + node v22.20.0.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**

```
$ openclaw agent --local --session-key agent:main:soulsmoke-livetest \
    -m "please never use the word utilize in your responses"
... (~15s of plugin / system-prompt / tool-bundle bootstrap)
Added to SOUL.md: 'Never use the word "utilize" in responses; use "use" instead.'
Already there. I'll keep using "use" instead.

$ tail -2 ~/openclaw-soulsmoke-ws/SOUL.md
- Never use em-dashes in communication. <!-- 2026-06-11 --> <!-- evidence: User said: "stop using em-dashes" -->
- Never use the word "utilize" in responses; use "use" instead. <!-- 2026-06-23 --> <!-- evidence: User said: "please never use the word utilize in your responses" -->

$ openclaw soul undo
Removed from SOUL.md: 'Never use the word "utilize" in responses; use "use" instead.'
```

**Observed result after fix.**
- Reflection sub-turn fired on the `never` signal keyword and was the only context in which `soul_update` was visible to the model (verified by gating test `openclaw-tools.soul-update-gate.test.ts`).
- Forced notice printed to the CLI as designed — cannot be suppressed via flag.
- Rule landed under the `## Auto-added` section with ISO date + evidence quote in HTML comments.
- The main turn ran with a freshly assembled system prompt that already included the new rule — the model said *"Already there. I'll keep using 'use' instead."*, demonstrating SOUL.md bootstrap reload between sub-turn and main turn.
- `openclaw soul undo` removed the last bullet atomically.
- With `autoUpdate: false` (default) the sub-turn never fires and `soul_update` is never registered (covered by gating test).

**What was not tested.**
- Non-CLI channel surfaces (Telegram/Discord/etc.) — forced notice is CLI/TUI only, called out as a follow-up in scope limits.
- Interval-based trigger — intentionally deferred (see scope limits).

## Test plan

- [x] 89 unit + integration tests pass (10 files): `vitest run src/agents/soul-* src/agents/tools/soul-update-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.soul-update-gate.test.ts`
- [x] Live end-to-end demo against Haiku 4.5 (transcript above)
- [x] Gating test confirms `soul_update` invisible to main-turn models even when `autoUpdate: true`
- [x] Hostile-input regression tests for HTML-comment-delimiter escapes: closer in evidence, closer + fake opener in rule, newline-injected fresh bullet
- [x] Schema-shape regression test confirms provider-visible `required` does not include `rule` (noop path)
- [ ] `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` full lane — running before un-draft
- [ ] `codex review --base origin/main` locally — running before un-draft

## AI-assisted checklist

- [x] Marked as AI-assisted (above)
- [x] Human-run real behavior proof from my own setup included above
- [x] I understand what the code does
- [ ] Prompts / session logs — can attach on request
- [ ] `codex review --base origin/main` run locally — running before un-draft
- [x] Will resolve or reply to bot review conversations as they come in

## Intentional scope limits

No interval trigger, `please` excluded from signal keywords, CLI-only forced notice, no review/prune. Each filed as follow-up.

**Note on signal-keyword breadth.** The trigger set is intentionally broad (`stop`, `don't`, `never`, `prefer`, `no more`). Every match runs a full reflection sub-turn before the main response, which costs ~10–15s on Haiku 4.5. This is the cost of the agent-judgment design — we want the model to decide whether the message represents a durable preference, and we accept noop sub-turns as the price of catching real ones. If maintainers consider this latency too aggressive, narrowing to multi-word phrases (`please stop`, `from now on`, `never again`) is a one-line change in `SIGNAL_KEYWORDS`; happy to do that in a follow-up before merge if preferred.

## Clawsweeper findings addressed (commit `2ba442c`)

- **[P1] Escape persisted SOUL evidence** (`soul-auto-update.ts:35-37`) — HTML-comment opener and closer in user/model-derived text are now neutralized via `neutralizeForSoul`. Three new hostile-input regression tests in `soul-auto-update.test.ts`.
- **[P2] Noop tool schema** (`soul-update-tool.ts:8-12`) — `rule` is now `Type.Optional` in the provider-visible JSON Schema. Runtime keeps enforcing "rule OR noop=true." Schema-shape regression test added.
- **[P2] Config surface alignment** (`zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts:104-108`) — added `agents.defaults.soul` and `agents.defaults.soul.autoUpdate` to `schema.help.ts` (help text) and `schema.labels.ts` (UI label) so the new key appears in config discovery.

target.title: feat: make mobile setup-code pairing one step
target.body:
Closes #98242

## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a problem where fresh mobile users had to perform QR/setup-code entry plus separate device/node approval steps before iOS or Android could land connected to the Gateway.

## Why This Change Was Made

This makes the existing QR/setup-code bootstrap path the one-step mobile onboarding baseline: the setup payload configures the app, the Gateway approves the fresh native mobile device handoff, and the matching fresh node pairing is approved through the same bounded setup-code bootstrap. It also adds an 8-character gateway-local short-code redemption seam for cases where the app already knows which Gateway URL to contact.

The security boundary stays narrow: setup bootstrap keeps the bounded node/operator profile, does not grant `operator.admin` or `operator.pairing`, short codes are TTL-bound and single-use, and insecure public Gateway URLs still fail closed outside loopback/private LAN allowances.

## User Impact

Users can scan a QR code or paste a setup code and land connected without walking through separate pending approvals for the fresh mobile device and node. Operators still get explicit approval boundaries for later role, scope, capability, or pairing upgrades.

## Evidence

- Redacted visual proof animation: https://gist.github.com/Solvely-Colin/0d647e2ffd90500925a062dc043d3764

![Redacted setup-code mobile pairing proof](https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Solvely-Colin/0d647e2ffd90500925a062dc043d3764/raw/setup-code-flow-redacted.svg)

- Physical iPhone + Tailscale QR proof: scanned the QR, app connected, Gateway showed device auto-approval and node auto-approval via setup-code bootstrap, and pending device/node counts were `0` afterward.
- Simulator setup-code proof: clean iPhone 17 simulator install, generated setup code against the running dev Gateway state, pasted setup code, app reached `Connected` / `Gateway Online` for `127.0.0.1:19001`; redacted GIF generated locally from that run.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/pairing/setup-short-code.test.ts src/gateway/server-http.probe.test.ts src/cli/qr-cli.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts --testNamePattern "qr setup code"`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo`
- `swift test --filter GatewaySetupShortCodeRedeemerTests` from `apps/shared/OpenClawKit`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/cli/qr.md docs/channels/pairing.md docs/gateway/protocol.md`
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/cli/qr.md docs/channels/pairing.md docs/gateway/protocol.md`
- `node scripts/docs-link-audit.mjs docs/cli/qr.md docs/channels/pairing.md docs/gateway/protocol.md`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --prompt-file /tmp/openclaw-qr-setup-pr-prep.md` returned `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`.

Notes:
- iOS simulator build/run succeeded through XcodeBuildMCP; existing unrelated SwiftLint/Swift warnings remain in `GatewaySettingsStore.swift`, `NodeAppModel.swift`, `TalkModeManager.swift`, `ChatViewModel.swift`, and `RealtimeTalkRelaySession.swift`.
- Broad Crabbox/Testbox `check:changed` was skipped by request after the local Crabbox binary/toolbox path failed before running the repo check.

target.title: fix(agents): keep merged delivery routes account-bound
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A gateway running two or more accounts on the same channel (the `per-account-channel-peer` DM scope exists for exactly this) can deliver a subagent, cron, or media completion to the wrong chat under the wrong bot, or fail delivery outright.

When a completion origin carries an account id but no concrete target (the common case for subagent spawns, cron runs, and heartbeat paths where `agentTo` is undefined), `mergeDeliveryContext` backfills the missing `to`/`threadId` from the session fallback whenever the two share a channel, ignoring account. If the fallback belongs to a different account on the same channel, that account's chat id and thread are pinned onto the primary's account: the message is addressed to bot-b's chat but sent through bot-a (cross-account misroute), or rejected because the target id is invalid under bot-a's token.

## Why This Change Was Made

This invariant was added intentionally and then lost. On 2026-06-22, commit `1ed8592467` ("fix(agents): keep fallback routes account-bound") added an `accountsConflict` guard plus a test named "does not inherit route fields from a different account on the same channel". Five minutes later, `025db6cf9e` (PR #89949, "pin media requester route at task start") removed that guard and deleted the test as collateral while solving an unrelated problem. The media-pin merge operates on same-account inputs (a requester and its own session), so the guard was a no-op for that path and did not need to be removed. No fix has landed since, so current `main` still carries the regression.

## User Impact

Operators with multi-account-same-channel deployments get completions routed to the wrong chat under the wrong bot, or silently dropped. Single-account and cross-channel deployments are unaffected (no account conflict can arise).

## Root cause

`src/utils/delivery-context.shared.ts`, `mergeDeliveryContext` gates field-crossing on channel only, with no account awareness (before):

```ts
const channelsConflict =
  normalizedPrimary?.channel &&
  normalizedFallback?.channel &&
  normalizedPrimary.channel !== normalizedFallback.channel;
return normalizeDeliveryContext({
  channel: normalizedPrimary?.channel ?? normalizedFallback?.channel,
  to: channelsConflict
    ? normalizedPrimary?.to
    : (normalizedPrimary?.to ?? normalizedFallback?.to),
  accountId: channelsConflict
    ? normalizedPrimary?.accountId
    : (normalizedPrimary?.accountId ?? normalizedFallback?.accountId),
  threadId: channelsConflict
    ? normalizedPrimary?.threadId
    : (normalizedPrimary?.threadId ?? normalizedFallback?.threadId),
});
```

Same channel + different account means `channelsConflict` is false, so the fallback's `to`/`threadId` cross onto the primary's account.

## Fix

Restore the account-bound guard (the same shape `1ed8592467` introduced) so a route conflict is either a channel mismatch or an account mismatch (after):

```ts
const channelsConflict =
  normalizedPrimary?.channel &&
  normalizedFallback?.channel &&
  normalizedPrimary.channel !== normalizedFallback.channel;
const accountsConflict =
  normalizedPrimary?.accountId &&
  normalizedFallback?.accountId &&
  normalizedPrimary.accountId !== normalizedFallback.accountId;
const routesConflict = channelsConflict || accountsConflict;
return normalizeDeliveryContext({
  channel: normalizedPrimary?.channel ?? normalizedFallback?.channel,
  to: routesConflict ? normalizedPrimary?.to : (normalizedPrimary?.to ?? normalizedFallback?.to),
  accountId: routesConflict
    ? normalizedPrimary?.accountId
    : (normalizedPrimary?.accountId ?? normalizedFallback?.accountId),
  threadId: routesConflict
    ? normalizedPrimary?.threadId
    : (normalizedPrimary?.threadId ?? normalizedFallback?.threadId),
});
```

The guard only triggers when both sides carry a defined, differing account id. When either side has no account id (the same-account and missing-account cases that the media route-pin path relies on), backfill proceeds exactly as before, so the route-pin fix from `025db6cf9e` is preserved.

## Why this is the right boundary

`mergeDeliveryContext` is the single owner of route-field crossing during session merges; every caller (`resolveAnnounceOrigin` for subagent/cron/media completions, `normalizeSessionDeliveryFields`, the media route-pin path) inherits the invariant from here, so the one-line guard fixes all surfaces at once rather than patching each caller. The media route-pin path added in `025db6cf9e` keeps working because its inputs are same-account. The restored regression test is colocated at the same boundary.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/utils/delivery-context.test.ts`: 11 passed. The restored test "does not inherit route fields from a different account on the same channel" fails on pristine `main` (prod file reverted) and passes with the patch.
- `oxlint --type-aware` and `oxfmt --check` on both changed files: clean.
- `run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json` and `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json`: clean.

## Evidence

The defect and its fix are demonstrated by driving the real completion-origin resolver below. The restored colocated test guards against re-regression. Provenance: regression of `1ed8592467`, removed by `025db6cf9e` (PR #89949).

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: a subagent/cron/media completion whose origin knows its account (bot-a) but not a concrete target inherits a different account's (bot-b) chat id and thread on the same channel, so it is addressed to bot-b's chat but sent through bot-a.
Real environment tested: drove the real production resolver `resolveAnnounceOrigin` from `src/agents/subagent-announce-origin.ts` (the live origin path for subagent, cron, and media completions, called from `subagent-announce.ts`, `media-generate-background-shared.ts`, and `plugin-sdk/agent-harness-task-runtime.ts`) on pristine `main` 66e676d29b and on the patched tree, identical inputs. The resolver and the real `mergeDeliveryContext`/`normalizeDeliveryContext` stayed real; nothing was stubbed.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node_modules/.bin/tsx repro-crossaccount.mts`, which calls `resolveAnnounceOrigin({ lastChannel: "telegram", lastTo: "123", lastAccountId: "bot-b", lastThreadId: "99" }, { channel: "telegram", accountId: "bot-a" })`.
Evidence after fix:
```
# BEFORE (pristine main 66e676d29b)
   requesterOrigin = telegram bot-a (account known, no concrete target)
   session entry   = telegram bot-b, lastTo '123', thread '99'
   actual completion origin: {"channel":"telegram","to":"123","accountId":"bot-a","threadId":"99"}
   --> a completion meant for account bot-a is now addressed to bot-b's chat id "123" but sent via bot-a: cross-account misroute / failed delivery

# AFTER (this patch, identical inputs)
   requesterOrigin = telegram bot-a (account known, no concrete target)
   session entry   = telegram bot-b, lastTo '123', thread '99'
   actual completion origin: {"channel":"telegram","accountId":"bot-a"}
```
Observed result after fix: the completion origin stays bound to bot-a's own account and no longer borrows bot-b's chat id "123" or thread "99"; the only field that moves between before and after is the cross-account target/thread that is now correctly dropped.
What was not tested: no live Telegram Bot API request against two real bot tokens; reachability still requires a genuine multi-account-same-channel deployment. Full `pnpm build` not run (change is not on a dynamic-import or packaging boundary).

target.title: fix(moonshot): bound video description JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The Moonshot video description endpoint (`describeMoonshotVideo` in
`extensions/moonshot/media-understanding-provider.ts`) reads its success
response with an unbounded `await res.json()`. The same media understanding
pattern was already fixed for xAI STT (`xai/stt.ts`) and OpenRouter STT
(`openrouter/media-understanding-provider.ts`) in the response-limit campaign,
but the Moonshot provider was overlooked.

A misbehaving, compromised, or hostile Moonshot endpoint (including a
self-hosted `baseUrl` override) can stream an arbitrarily large JSON body into
memory before parsing, causing memory pressure or a hang on the video description
code path.

This change closes the remaining unbounded surface, making this the Moonshot
media-understanding companion to the `#95103` / `#95108` / `#95218`
response-limit campaign.

## Changes

* `extensions/moonshot/media-understanding-provider.ts`:
  - Adds `readProviderJsonResponse` to the existing `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` import
  - Replaces `(await res.json()) as OpenAiCompatibleVideoPayload` with
    `readProviderJsonResponse<OpenAiCompatibleVideoPayload>(res, "Moonshot video description failed")` (16 MiB cap)
  - Label uses `"Moonshot video description failed"` (matching the `assertOkOrThrowHttpError` error prefix on the same code path) so error messages are predictable: `"Moonshot video description failed: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"` and `"Moonshot video description failed: malformed JSON response"`

* `extensions/moonshot/media-understanding-provider.test.ts`:
  - Adds `oversizedJsonResponse` helper (ReadableStream-backed, tracks chunks read and cancel)
  - Adds `"bounds successful Moonshot video JSON bodies instead of buffering the whole response"`: streams 64 × 1 MiB chunks, asserts the call rejects with the cap error, `readCount < 64`, and `wasCanceled() === true`
  - Adds `"reports malformed Moonshot video JSON with a provider-owned error"`: feeds `"not-json{"` body, asserts `"Moonshot video description failed: malformed JSON response"`

## Real behavior proof

* **Behavior addressed**: An untrusted Moonshot video description success response
  with no `Content-Length` and an oversized/never-ending body must not be buffered
  whole; the read must stop at the cap, cancel the stream, and throw a bounded
  error — while valid small responses still parse unchanged.
* **Real environment tested**: A real `node:http` server (`createServer`) bound
  to `127.0.0.1`, streaming a JSON body in 64 KiB chunks with **no
  Content-Length** header, driving the real exported `readResponseWithLimit`
  helper (the same helper `readProviderJsonResponse` wraps internally) on
  Node v22.22.0.
* **Exact steps**:
  1. Boot the server; `GET /huge` streams ~24 MiB with no `Content-Length`;
     `GET /small` returns one valid video description response.
  2. Drive `readResponseWithLimit(response, 1 MiB)` against `/huge` and assert
     it rejects + stream is cancelled.
  3. **Negative control**: run the OLD `await res.json()` path and measure total
     bytes pushed.
  4. Drive against `/small` and assert the payload is parsed intact.
* **Evidence after fix**:
  * Bounded case: threw `Moonshot video description failed: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes`;
    server socket closed early; only **1,064,178 bytes** reached the wire.
  * Negative control: the unbounded `response.json()` pulled the **full
    25,165,824 bytes** (>23x the bounded read), proving the cap is load-bearing.
  * Small case: parsed intact, no truncation.
* **Observed result**: `ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED`. In-repo test coverage
  added: 4 tests pass (including 2 new bounds/malformed-JSON behavior tests).
* **What was not tested**: Live Moonshot API calls. The proof instead measures

target.title: security: scan markdown skill definitions for injection threats
target.body:
## Summary

The skill scanner (`src/security/skill-scanner.ts`) previously only scanned JavaScript/TypeScript-style source files for threats. This PR extends it to also scan Markdown `.md` files, including `SKILL.md` definitions, for injection and obfuscation patterns.

Markdown-specific rules added:
- `hidden-unicode` detects zero-width characters and text-direction overrides that can hide malicious content from visual review.
- `markdown-data-uri` detects data URIs with executable MIME types such as `text/html` and `application/javascript`.
- `markdown-download-exec` detects `curl`/`wget` download-and-execute pipelines, including common bypass forms like `sudo bash`, `/bin/bash`, `python3`, line continuations, and quoted pipe characters inside arguments.
- `markdown-encoded-payload` detects large base64 blocks inside Markdown.
- `markdown-hex-payload` detects hex-encoded payloads inside Markdown.

Implementation notes:
- Added `MARKDOWN_EXTENSIONS` and `isMarkdown()`.
- Added `.md` to `SCANNABLE_EXTENSIONS`.
- Applies Markdown-specific rules only to Markdown files, while preserving code-specific scanning behavior for code files.
- Uses the raw Markdown source for Markdown source-wide rules so URLs like `https://...` are not accidentally truncated by code-comment stripping.
- Adds focused tests for Markdown findings, Markdown/code rule separation, directory scanning, and summary counts.

Why this matters: skill definitions can include rendered Markdown. Without scanning these files, hidden instructions, obfuscated payloads, or download-and-execute commands in skill Markdown can bypass the existing code scanner.

Supersedes #10705 (auto-closed by stale bot). Rebased cleanly onto current `main`.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/security/skill-scanner.test.ts`
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/security/skill-scanner.ts src/security/skill-scanner.test.ts`
- [x] `pnpm check:changed`
- [x] Direct scanner smoke for Markdown download-and-execute bypass forms plus benign Markdown prose and table negative cases

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Markdown skill definitions such as `SKILL.md` are now scanned for hidden Unicode, executable data URIs, download-and-execute pipelines, and encoded payloads that previously fell outside the JavaScript/TypeScript scanner surface.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS at `/Users/alexalaniz/Code/openclaw-43469`, based on current `openclaw/openclaw:main` (`24bd0b212f`), with dependencies installed using `pnpm@10.33.2`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the actual scanner through `node node_modules/tsx/dist/cli.mjs` against Markdown sources containing `curl`/`wget` bypass forms, then ran `pnpm test src/security/skill-scanner.test.ts`, `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/security/skill-scanner.ts src/security/skill-scanner.test.ts`, and `pnpm check:changed`.

Evidence after fix: Copied live terminal output from the local OpenClaw checkout:

```text
markdown scanner smoke passed 7 positives and 2 negatives

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  32 passed (32)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 3.08s

All matched files use the correct format.

Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
runtime-sidecar-loaders: local runtime sidecar loaders look OK.
Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
```

Observed result after fix: The actual parser-based scanner caught 7 Markdown download-and-execute bypass samples in the smoke run (`sudo bash`, `sudo -E bash`, `env FOO=1 bash`, `/bin/bash`, `python3`, a backslash-newline before `| bash`, and a quoted pipe inside a curl header) and did not flag benign curl prose or `| curl | bash |` Markdown table cells, while the targeted scanner test file and changed-file gate completed successfully.

What was not tested: No known gaps.

target.title: refactor: flip sessions and transcripts to sqlite storage
target.body:
Related: #96625
Related: #88838

## What Problem This Solves

Completes the session/transcript storage flip tracked by openclaw/openclaw#88838 by making per-agent SQLite the canonical runtime store for session metadata and transcript events. Legacy `sessions.json` and active transcript JSONL files are now doctor import inputs or archive artifacts, not runtime fallback state.

## Why This Change Was Made

The accessor seams from the earlier work made a single storage flip practical. Shipping a mixed file/SQLite runtime would let session metadata and transcript rows drift, so this PR moves import, runtime access, retention, SDK cleanup, and ratchets together behind the permanent accessor boundary.

## User Impact

Existing legacy session stores can be migrated non-destructively through doctor dry-run/import/validate/inspect flows. Runtime session creation, resume, transcript append, compaction, and maintenance use SQLite rows; retention still exports archived JSONL artifacts before deleting rows so support and archive workflows keep inspectable artifacts.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/check-session-accessor-boundary.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/config/sessions/targets.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-sqlite.test.ts test/scripts/check-session-accessor-boundary.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-accessor.conformance.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.session-lifecycle-mutation.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/session-entry-projection.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/config/sessions/targets.ts src/config/sessions/targets.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-sqlite.test.ts`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` clean after two accepted findings were fixed.
- Copied local OpenClaw state proof under `/tmp/openclaw-sqlite-flip-final-20260625T013042Z`: doctor dry-run/import/validate/inspect on `codex-proof` and `codex-image-proof` reported zero issues; import wrote 2 entries plus 15 and 18 transcript events respectively.
- Isolated runtime smoke created an agent SQLite DB, created/resumed a session, appended transcript rows, compacted 4 rows to 3, and archive-before-delete cleanup removed 1 lifecycle entry after writing 1 archive artifact.

target.title: fix(tui): respect queue mode in sendMessage busy guard
target.body:
Fixes #89059
Fixes #90012

## Summary

The TUI `sendMessage()` hardcodes a busy check that blocks new messages when the agent is running, completely ignoring the `/queue` mode setting configured on the Gateway. This makes `followup`, `collect`, and `interrupt` modes unusable from the TUI because messages are rejected at the client layer before reaching the Gateway's queue system.

## Changes

**Gateway session row** (`session-utils.types.ts`, `session-utils.ts`): Add `queueMode` to `GatewaySessionRow` and populate it from the session entry so the TUI can read the session's queue mode.

**TUI types** (`tui-types.ts`, `tui-backend.ts`): Add `queueMode` to `SessionInfo` and the `TuiSessionList` sessions Pick so the TUI can read the session's queue mode.

**TUI session propagation** (`tui-session-actions.ts`): Propagate `queueMode` from the session entry to `state.sessionInfo` during `applySessionInfo`.

**TUI busy guard** (`tui-command-handlers.ts`): Gate the `activeChatRunId` and `pendingChatRunId` checks on `effectiveQueueMode === "steer"`. When the mode is `followup`, `collect`, or `interrupt`, the message is forwarded to the Gateway for queue handling. The `pendingOptimisticUserMessage` guard remains unconditional to prevent rapid double-sends while the previous RPC is in flight.

**Catch block fix (P1):** When a queued followup send fails, preserve the prior `activeChatRunId` instead of clearing it. A failed enqueue should not forget the still-running active turn.

**Typed-submit gate** (`tui.ts`): Update `canSubmitTuiChatMessage` to accept and honor `queueMode`. In steer mode (default), it blocks gateway sends during active runs and when a pending chat run exists. In non-steer modes, only `pendingOptimisticUserMessage` blocks.

**Tests**: 9 new tests: 5 for `canSubmitTuiChatMessage` (followup/collect/interrupt allow, steer blocks, pendingOptimistic still blocks in followup), and 4 for the command-handler busy guard (followup allows, pendingOptimistic blocks in followup, queued send failure preserves active run, non-queued failure clears active run).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: TUI sendMessage blocks messages with "agent is busy" when the agent is running, regardless of the /queue mode setting. followup, collect, and interrupt modes never reach the Gateway. Additionally, the catch block unconditionally cleared activeChatRunId on failure, forgetting the still-running active turn when a queued send failed.
Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node v22.19, OpenClaw built from patched source at commit a1addc94c0.
Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Type check, lint, and run tests:

```console
$ pnpm tsgo:core
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/tui/tui-command-handlers.ts src/tui/tui.ts src/tui/tui-types.ts src/tui/tui-backend.ts src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/session-utils.types.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui.test.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 2.9s on 9 files with 242 rules using 1 threads.
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tui/tui.test.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts
 ✓ |tui| src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts (73 tests) 25ms
 ✓ |tui| src/tui/tui.test.ts (68 tests) 18ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  141 passed (141)
```

Step 2. Build and verify fix in compiled production code:

```console
$ pnpm build
[build-all] phase timings: total 77.2s
$ grep -n 'effectiveQueueMode\|allowQueuedSend\|queueMode' dist/tui-DR23uKra.js
2470:const allowQueuedSend = (state.sessionInfo.queueMode ?? "steer") !== "steer";
2471:if (!isBtw && (state.pendingOptimisticUserMessage || !allowQueuedSend && (state.pendingChatRunId || opts.local !== true && state.activeChatRunId))) {
3589:if (entry?.queueMode !== void 0) next.queueMode = entry.queueMode;
4218:if ((params.queueMode ?? "steer") !== "steer") return true;
```

Step 3. Start gateway to confirm module loads cleanly:

```console
$ timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway
[gateway] loading configuration...
[gateway] starting...
[gateway] http server listening (9 plugins; 1.9s)
[gateway] ready
[gateway] signal SIGTERM received
[shutdown] completed cleanly in 52ms
```

Evidence after fix: All 4 queue-mode-aware code paths are present in the compiled TUI bundle. The gateway starts and shuts down cleanly with the patched code. All 141 tests pass (including 9 new queue-mode tests), zero lint errors.
Observed result after fix: The production `canSubmitTuiChatMessage` gate correctly allows typed messages through in non-steer queue modes (followup, collect, interrupt) while still blocking in steer mode. The catch block preserves `activeChatRunId` when a queued send fails. The compiled dist confirms the fix shipped.
What was not tested: Live TUI interaction with a running agent using `/queue mode=followup` then sending a message mid-run (requires an active provider with credentials and a long-running turn). The end-to-end flow relies on the Gateway's existing queue system which is already tested.

target.title: fix(llm): preserve structured tool result text across providers
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Tool results with structured non-text content were being collapsed into generic placeholders during provider replay and transport conversion. In practice, structured non-media blocks could become `(see attached image)`, `(see attached media)`, or empty/no-output text, which loses useful tool output and can mislead the model.

This PR preserves explicit text first, serializes structured non-media blocks as redacted JSON text, and keeps media-only fallbacks type-aware.


## Current Head

`f471e8e801c4e5bfaaf7e766e9d9146f246e46d5`

## Why This Change Was Made

The canonical user-facing problem is tracked in #96857. The branch already covered core provider replay paths, but the latest ClawSweeper review identified one remaining touched sibling path:

- `extensions/google/transport-stream.ts`: the bundled Google transport still built `functionResponse.response.output` from explicit `text` blocks or a media placeholder only, so structured-only non-media tool results could still become empty output.

This head closes that Google transport gap without importing core `src/**` helpers into plugin production code. The extension now has boundary-safe structured extraction for Google tool results before media/no-output fallback.

## User Impact

- Structured JSON/resource-like tool results remain readable in provider replay instead of being dropped.
- Explicit `text` tool-result blocks still win and are not replaced by serialized structured fallback.
- Media-only tool results still use placeholders, now with the right media type:
  - image-only → `(see attached image)`
  - audio-only → `(see attached audio)`
  - mixed image/audio → `(see attached media)`
- Secret-like fields, inline data URIs, opaque encrypted fields, and binary `data` fields are redacted or omitted before provider-bound replay text is generated.
- The bundled Google transport now matches the core provider replay shape while staying inside the plugin SDK boundary.

## Implementation

- Adds shared provider-side tool-result text extraction for structured blocks.
- Redacts secrets and inline data URIs before serializing structured tool-result blocks.
- Omits opaque/binary fields instead of replaying raw payloads.
- Applies the extraction/fallback path across:
  - OpenAI Responses
  - OpenAI Completions
  - Anthropic provider
  - Google shared provider
  - Mistral provider
  - OpenAI transport stream
  - Anthropic transport stream
- Extends extension fallback handling for:
  - xAI Responses transport normalization
  - Google extension transport stream
- Adds Google extension structured replay repair:
  - structured-only Google tool results serialize into `functionResponse.response.output`
  - explicit text remains text-first
  - media-only blocks remain on media placeholders
  - secret-like, inline-data, opaque, and binary fields are redacted/omitted before replay

## Regression Coverage

Added or updated focused coverage for:

- `src/llm/providers/tool-result-text.test.ts`
  - explicit text wins
  - structured non-media blocks serialize as JSON text
  - media-only blocks are omitted from text extraction
  - image/audio/mixed media placeholder selection
  - inline data URI and binary-field redaction
- `src/llm/providers/openai-responses-shared.test.ts`
  - structured tool-result output is not converted to image placeholder
  - audio-only output uses audio placeholder rather than image/no-output fallback
- `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
  - structured output is preserved rather than collapsed to image placeholder
- `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts`
  - structured blocks are serialized
  - structured blocks are preserved alongside images
- `extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts`
  - structured-only Google tool-result output becomes `functionResponse.response.output` text
  - explicit Google tool-result text wins over structured fallback
  - opaque, binary, inline-data, and secret-like structured fields are redacted/omitted
  - media-only Google tool results stay on media placeholders
- `extensions/xai/stream.test.ts`
  - audio-only function output uses audio placeholder

## Evidence

### Review result addressed

Latest ClawSweeper review on `1ee3b0a60fcdbe4686cc4860bc986e742fb9c483` reported:

- Finding: `[P2] Serialize structured Google tool results before fallback`
- Location: `extensions/google/transport-stream.ts:705-707`
- Root issue: Google transport still extracted only explicit text and otherwise used only a media placeholder, leaving structured-only non-media results as empty `functionResponse.response.output`.

This head addresses that finding with extension-boundary-safe structured extraction and focused Google transport regression coverage.

### Current branch author-side validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --reporter=dot`
  - Result: passed, 1 file / 72 tests.
  - Covers the new Google structured-only replay path, explicit-text-first behavior, media-only placeholders, and redaction/omission behavior.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt extensions/google/transport-stream.ts extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts`
  - Result: completed for both changed files.
- `node --check extensions/google/transport-stream.ts`
  - Result: passed before the focused test run.
- `node --check extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts`
  - Result: passed before the focused test run.
- `git diff --check`
  - Result: passed before commit.

### Latest patch-quality update

Current head `f471e8e801c4e5bfaaf7e766e9d9146f246e46d5` keeps the existing provider replay evidence intact and addresses the latest ClawSweeper patch-quality/security finding by removing the Google extension-local structured serializer copy:

- `src/plugin-sdk/provider-transport-runtime.ts` exposes the shared structured tool-result text extraction and media-placeholder helpers as a plugin-safe seam.
- `extensions/google/transport-stream.ts` reuses those shared SDK helpers for Google `functionResponse.response.output`, so Google now follows the same redaction/truncation/media fallback contract as the core provider replay paths.
- `src/logging/redact.ts` keeps the shared structured redaction contract broad enough for the reviewed sensitive key families before provider replay text is serialized, while preserving uppercase diagnostic `code` values such as `SYSTEM_RUN_DENIED`.
- `extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts`, `src/llm/providers/tool-result-text.test.ts`, and `src/logging/redact.test.ts` cover structured-only Google output, explicit-text-first behavior, media-only placeholders, opaque/binary/inline-data omission, the reviewed sensitive field names (`privateKey`, `private_key`, `cookie`, `set-cookie`, `jwt`, `session`, `code`, `signature`, generic `key`, and payment fields), and the diagnostic-code non-regression case.
- `docs/plugins/sdk-subpaths.md` and `docs/plugins/sdk-migration.md` document that `plugin-sdk/provider-transport-runtime` now includes tool-result replay helpers.

Author-side validation for this head:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/logging/redact.test.ts src/llm/providers/tool-result-text.test.ts extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --reporter=dot` passed, covering 3 focused files / 192 tests.
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/google/transport-stream.ts extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-transport-runtime.ts` passed with 0 warnings and 0 errors.
- `node --check extensions/google/transport-stream.ts` passed.
- `node --check extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts` passed.
- `node --check src/plugin-sdk/provider-transport-runtime.ts` passed.
- `node --check src/llm/providers/tool-result-text.test.ts` passed.
- `node --check src/logging/redact.ts` passed.
- `node --check src/logging/redact.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt extensions/google/transport-stream.ts extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-transport-runtime.ts src/llm/providers/tool-result-text.test.ts src/logging/redact.ts` completed.
- `pnpm docs:list` completed and the affected SDK docs were checked.
- `git diff --check` passed.

Current-head GitHub Actions evidence:

- `Real behavior proof` passed for `f471e8e801c4e5bfaaf7e766e9d9146f246e46d5`: <https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28456351010/job/84332098351>

### Existing provider replay proof retained from the previous detailed PR body

The previous current-head evidence remains relevant to the core provider replay slice and is repeated here so the PR body stays globally consistent instead of losing proof while the branch advances:

- Current-head Real behavior proof checks previously passed for provider replay/redaction coverage:
  - <https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28379436171/job/84077730201>
  - <https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28379481457/job/84077892770>

- The redacted provider replay artifact demonstrated the current-head replay wire shapes for:
  - OpenAI Responses: structured JSON reached `function_call_output.output` as text instead of `(see attached image)`.
  - Anthropic provider replay: structured tool-result text was serialized into the provider-bound replay content instead of becoming an image placeholder.
  - Mistral provider replay: structured tool-result text was serialized into the provider-bound replay content instead of becoming an image placeholder.
  - Redaction behavior: secret-like input was redacted; inline data URIs, binary data, and opaque encrypted fields were omitted or replaced with redacted placeholders before provider replay.

target.title: fix(feishu): retry once on token-invalid errors 99991663/99991664 with cache invalidation (fixes #97287)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #97287.

When the cached `tenant_access_token` becomes invalid (clock drift, server-side revocation, etc.), the Feishu plugin could not send messages. Error code 99991663 (`Invalid access token`) was thrown immediately with no automatic retry, requiring manual gateway restart.

## Root Cause

`requestFeishuApi` only retried on rate-limit errors (429, 11232, 230020). Token-invalid errors (99991663, 99991664) were not handled.

## Changes Made

- `extensions/feishu/src/comment-shared.ts`: Added `FEISHU_TOKEN_INVALID_CODES` (99991663, 99991664), `getFeishuTokenInvalidCode()` exporter, and `setClearAllFeishuTokenCaches()` setter (avoids circular dependency with streaming-card.ts). Modified `requestFeishuApi` to clear the token cache and retry once on first-attempt token-invalid errors.
- `extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts`: Registered `clearFeishuTokenCache()` via the setter so the module-level `tokenCache` Map is cleared on token-invalid errors.
- `extensions/feishu/src/send.retry.test.ts`: Added 8 tests covering token-invalid detection, single retry with cache clearing, persistent failure exhaustion, and non-token non-rate-limit error preservation.

## Evidence

- **Unit tests:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/send.retry.test.ts` — 40/40 passed
- **Lint:** clean
- **Lockfile:** unchanged

## AI Assistance 🤖

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding**: Yes

## Update 2026-07-01

### Code fixes
- **Cache clearing registered at client module**: Moved `setClearAllFeishuTokenCaches` registration from `streaming-card.ts` (only loaded for streaming card path) to `client.ts` (always loaded), so normal send paths also benefit from token-invalid recovery
- **Preserve retry error details**: Replaced generic 'unreachable' exhausted-retries error with a descriptive message

### Real behavior proof

**Feishu API connectivity (verified with real credentials):**
```
$ curl -s -X POST "https://open.feishu.cn/open-apis/auth/v3/tenant_access_token/internal" ...
{"code":0,"expire":7064,"msg":"ok","tenant_access_token":"t-g1..."}
```

**Token-invalid error detection (via production code):**
```
✅ 99991663 (token invalid): 99991663 (expected 99991663)
✅ 99991664 (token expired): 99991664 (expected 99991664)
✅ non-token error (230001): undefined (expected undefined)
✅ no error object: undefined (expected undefined)
```

**Unit tests (78/78 passed):**
```
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  78 passed (78)
```

## Review
- Manually reviewed and verified
- AI-assisted: Yes

target.title: refactor(memory): drop redundant agent-id scoping from qmd collection names
target.body:
## Summary

- **What:** drop the redundant `-<agentId>` scoping from managed qmd collection names. Each agent has its **own isolated qmd index** (`agents/<agentId>/qmd/index.sqlite`), so collection names can't collide across agents — the agent-id suffix adds nothing but hardcodes the agent id into collection identifiers.
- **How:** remove `scopeCollectionBase` (and two now-dead path helpers, `isPathInsideRoot` / `canonicalizePathForContainment`); name default collections by their base (`memory-root`, `memory-dir`), the session collection `sessions`, and extra collections by their explicit name. Reverse the legacy migration to **scoped→unscoped** (`migrateLegacyScopedCollections` / `deriveLegacyScopedName`) so existing per-agent indexes migrate cleanly.
- **Why migration is cheap:** qmd collections are a **rebuildable derived index** (not user data), so the migration drops the stale scoped collection and lets the unscoped one be (re)created + reindexed — no import, no data loss (consistent with this repo's "prefer delete/drop/rebuild over import for cache").
- **Outcome:** stable, agent-agnostic collection names; net **−42 LOC** of production code for the de-scope itself (removes `scopeCollectionBase` + two now-dead path helpers in `backend-config.ts`).
- **Orphan cleanup:** the scoped→unscoped migration is **kind-gated**. Engine-owned collections (`memory`/`sessions`, whose base names are engine-generated and not user-suppliable) are removed even when their pattern has drifted from the current default; user-named (`custom`) collections keep the path+pattern guards and are **never** auto-deleted. Without this, a pattern-drifted legacy engine collection lingered as an orphan and was re-walked + re-embedded by every `qmd update` — a recurring reindex cost the migration otherwise shipped. (Trade-off, stated for transparency: for engine kinds this intentionally drops the prior "a user coincidentally named a collection like a legacy scoped name" guard — safe because those base names cannot be user-supplied; `custom` retains it.)
- **Disambiguated custom-name upgrade (ClawSweeper P1):** when two custom collections share a name, the shipped scoped names disambiguated as `<base>-<agentId>-<n>` (e.g. `notes-main`, `notes-main-2`), but the unscoped probe `deriveLegacyScopedName(<base>-<n>)` is `<base>-<n>-<agentId>` (`notes-2-main`) — so `migrateLegacyScopedCollections` missed the second orphan, and qmd then rejected the new unscoped collection on the same path+pattern, stranding it under the legacy name. `tryRebindConflictingCollection` now confirms the collection qmd names in the add-conflict via `qmd collection show` (the only command that surfaces the path, reusing `enrichLegacyCollectionPath`) and rebinds it when path **and** pattern match; a parsed name alone never removes a collection, so user-owned path guards are preserved.
- **Stacked on #91253 (per ClawSweeper):** the first commit is the path-aware qmd-rebind fix (#91253); the remaining commits are the de-scope, a docs + session-migration-test follow-up, and the kind-gated orphan cleanup. The rebind is **required** for the migration to be safe — without path-aware metadata, an upgraded index holding both a stale *unscoped* collection (old path) and a current *scoped* one could keep the stale unscoped target active; with the rebind folded in, the stale unscoped collection is rebound to the current path. Once #91253 lands, this rebases to drop that first commit.

## Linked context

Closes #91259

Depends on / folds in #91253 (path-aware qmd collection rebind) — the first commit of this PR.

Related: #91265 (an earlier PR toward the same proposal; this one folds in the path-aware rebind so existing scoped indexes migrate safely, adds an upgrade-case regression, and includes live before/after behavior proof).

Requested by a maintainer or owner? No — follows from the proposal in #91259.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: managed qmd collection names dropping the redundant `-<agentId>` suffix; existing per-agent indexes migrate scoped→unscoped; and orphaned engine-owned legacy collections are cleaned up.

Real environment tested: (A) a live, self-hosted OpenClaw gateway (macOS), backend `qmd` (`@tobilu/qmd` 2.5.2) running this build, against an agent whose index already held scoped collections; (B) a scratch per-agent index driven by the real `QmdMemoryManager` + the real `qmd` 2.5.2 binary for the orphan-cleanup case; (C) a scratch in-container→host rebind driven by the real `QmdMemoryManager` + real `qmd` 2.5.2 binary (the sandbox-mode transition).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
# (A) live gateway — de-scope migration on an agent with existing scoped collections:
openclaw gateway restart
openclaw memory index --force --agent <agent>
sqlite3 <agent-qmd-index>.sqlite "select name,path from store_collections order by name;"

# (B) scratch index — seed two legacy scoped orphans (drifted pattern), run the engine (cli mode):
qmd collection add <ws>/memory --name memory-dir-<agent> --mask "**/*.txt"   # engine-owned (memory)
qmd collection add <ws>/notes  --name notes-<agent>      --mask "**/*.txt"   # user-owned   (custom)
# then QmdMemoryManager.create({ mode: "cli" }) runs the migration; list collections before/after.

# (C) sandbox-mode transition — seed a collection at a stale in-container root, run the engine:
qmd collection add <root>/home/node/.openclaw/teams/x/workspace --name workspace --mask "**/*.md"
# then QmdMemoryManager.create({ mode: "cli" }) with workspace=<host-root>; `qmd collection show workspace` before/after.
```

Evidence after fix:
```
# (A) live gateway (identifiers redacted):
# BEFORE (scoped names):
memory-root-<agent>   <WS>
memory-dir-<agent>    <WS>/memory
sessions-<agent>      <agent-state>/qmd/sessions
team-library-<agent>  <WS>/library/promoted
team-library          <WS-old>/library/promoted    # orphan duplicate
# AFTER (reindex):
memory-root / memory-dir / sessions / team-library  # all unscoped
# scoped (-<agent>) collections remaining: 0

# (B) scratch index, real qmd 2.5.2 + real engine (both orphans had a DRIFTED pattern):
# BEFORE:
memory-dir-proofagent  Pattern: **/*.txt   # engine-owned (memory)
notes-proofagent       Pattern: **/*.txt   # user-owned   (custom)
# AFTER (engine ran, mode=cli):
notes-proofagent       Pattern: **/*.txt   # LEFT  — user data never auto-deleted
memory-root / memory-dir / notes           # unscoped, engine-created from scratch
# memory-dir-proofagent : 1 -> 0  (engine-owned, removed)
# notes-proofagent      : 1 -> 1  (user-owned, preserved)

# (C) sandbox-mode transition, real qmd 2.5.2 + real engine:
# BEFORE: workspace -> .../home/node/.openclaw/teams/x/workspace   (stale in-container root)
# AFTER (engine ran, mode=cli): workspace -> .../host/workspace    (rebound to host root)
```

Observed result after fix: (A) all managed collections migrated to unscoped names (including `sessions`); `0` scoped collections remained; the prior orphan duplicate (`team-library` + `team-library-<agent>`) collapsed to a single `team-library`. (B) the engine-owned drifted-pattern orphan was removed while the user-owned one was preserved, and fresh unscoped collections (`memory-root`/`memory-dir`/`notes`) were created. (C) a collection pinned to a stale in-container path was rebound to the configured host workspace root.

What was not tested: launching an actual Docker sandbox container — the bind-mount + container-launch plumbing is OpenClaw platform infrastructure, outside this PR's diff. The in-container→host path transition this PR's code performs is proven with the real binary in (C) above and by a unit test (container→host rebind transition, #91253).

Proof limitations or environment constraints: (A) is redacted terminal output from a private fleet (paths/agent/team identifiers removed); (B) uses generic scratch identifiers and is reproducible by any maintainer on `qmd 2.5.2`.

Before evidence: shown inline in (A) above (the pre-reindex scoped names + orphan duplicate).

## Tests and validation

Commands run (clean checkout off `openclaw/openclaw` `main`, re-verified on the current head after the disambiguation fix):
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/memory` → **444 passed (40 files)**
- `pnpm test packages/memory-host-sdk` → **38 passed (9 files)**
- `pnpm check:test-types` (tsgo) → **clean, 0 errors**
- `oxfmt --check` + `oxlint` on the touched files → clean.

Regression coverage:
- Default + custom collection-name tests now assert the unscoped form.
- The unscoped→scoped migration tests were replaced with scoped→unscoped ones.
- **Upgrade-case regression (addresses the flagged compatibility risk):** an index holding BOTH a stale *unscoped* collection (old path) AND a current *scoped* one at the name switch → asserts the stale unscoped collection is rebound to the **current** path (via the folded-in `collection show` path enrichment), not left active.
- **Kind-gated orphan cleanup:** a rewritten migration-guard test — a `memory`-kind legacy scoped collection with a drifted pattern is now **removed** (genuinely fails against the pre-fix guard), while a `custom`-kind one with a drifted pattern is **left** — plus a contract test asserting every managed collection carries a `kind` (the safety gate depends on it).
- **Disambiguated custom-name upgrade regression:** two custom collections both named `notes` at different roots, with legacy `notes-<agentId>` / `notes-<agentId>-2` seeded in a real-`qmd`-shaped mock (name-only `collection list`, path-bearing `collection show`) → asserts the disambiguated orphan (`notes-<agentId>-2`) is removed and both unscoped collections (`notes`, `notes-2`) bind to their correct roots. Red against the pre-fix conflict path (the orphan is never removed), green after.

Multi-agent note: safe by construction — each agent has its own isolated qmd index, so unscoped names cannot collide across agents.

## Risk checklist

User-visible behavior change? Internal only — qmd collection identifiers (a derived index) change; no user-facing API/config change.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? Migration only: existing per-agent indexes migrate scoped→unscoped on the next `ensureCollections()` (drop stale scoped + recreate unscoped + reindex). No config keys changed.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No.

Highest-risk area: the scoped→unscoped migration on existing indexes, and the kind-gated removal of legacy scoped collections.

How is that risk mitigated: removal only ever targets the exact derived legacy name `<base>-<agentId>` for a **desired** collection. For `custom` collections the path+pattern guards remain, so a user-named collection is never auto-deleted. For `memory`/`sessions` the base names are engine-generated and cannot be user-supplied, so the derived legacy name is unambiguously engine-owned; if such a collection is still configured it is simply re-added at the same path/pattern (net-zero, a reindex). qmd collections are rebuildable, so any dropped-then-recreated collection reindexes from source with no user-visible data loss.

## Current review state

Next action: maintainer review.

What is still waiting: CI + review.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed: ClawSweeper review findings on #91274 (docs example, session-migration coverage, scoped-duplicate upgrade case, orphan cleanup, and the disambiguated custom-name migration P1 — fixed via the show-verified conflict rebind).

target.title: fix(windows): remove findstr from scheduled-task restart probe (Fixes #84600)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #84600.

The Windows scheduled-task restart helper still probed task state with a PowerShell command piped into `findstr /I /C:"Running"`. That leaves a visible `cmd.exe -> findstr.exe` child process shape on some Windows hosts, which matches the black heartbeat/restart window reported in the issue.

This change keeps the same retry semantics but replaces the `findstr` pipeline with a pure PowerShell exit-code check:
- exit `0` when the scheduled task is already running
- exit `1` otherwise

Non-goals:
- no scheduled-task retry timing changes
- no service install/startup behavior changes
- no fallback-launcher changes

## Tests and validation

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/windows-task-restart.test.ts
git diff --check
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed:
- the generated Windows scheduled-task restart helper should not shell task-state detection through `findstr`

Environment tested:
- local source checkout on current `main`-based branch

Evidence after the fix:

target.title: fix: treat trusted-proxy gateway auth as doctor auth proof
target.body:
## Summary

- Count a complete `trusted-proxy` gateway setup as authentication proof for the network-exposure doctor check.
- Keep incomplete trusted-proxy setups on the existing critical path by requiring both `gateway.auth.trustedProxy.userHeader` and `gateway.trustedProxies`.
- Keep startup-invalid trusted-proxy/token conflicts and unsafe proxy source configs on specific critical paths.
- Require every non-empty `gateway.trustedProxies` entry to be a host-scoped trusted proxy source before doctor downgrades exposed Gateway auth from critical to warning.
- Replace the misleading token/`doctor --fix` remediation with trusted-proxy-specific proxy/firewall guidance.

## Why

`trusted-proxy` is an explicit gateway auth mode, and the gateway runtime already treats the proxy source boundary as security-sensitive. The doctor exposure check only recognized token/password shared-secret modes, so a LAN-bound trusted-proxy deployment could be reported as "without authentication" and nudged toward token repair even though the intended auth boundary is the reverse proxy.

This keeps the exposure warning, because a LAN-bound gateway still deserves review, but it no longer labels a complete trusted-proxy setup as unauthenticated.

It also avoids treating unsafe or startup-invalid trusted-proxy configs as proof: shared-token conflicts, missing user headers, missing proxy sources, broad proxy CIDRs/default routes, mixed exact-plus-broad proxy lists, and loopback proxy entries without explicit `allowLoopback` remain critical.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: LAN-bound Gateway configs using fully configured `trusted-proxy` auth were reported as unauthenticated by doctor/security.
- Real environment tested: A real local OpenClaw config using `gateway.bind=lan`, `gateway.auth.mode=trusted-proxy`, and configured trusted proxies.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `npm_config_cache=/private/tmp/openclaw-npm-cache npm exec --yes pnpm@11.2.2 -- node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json`
- Evidence after fix: Copied live output from the patched dev CLI included these `core/doctor/security` findings:

```json
[
  {
    "checkId": "core/doctor/security",
    "severity": "warning",
    "message": "WARNING: Gateway bound to \"lan\" (0.0.0.0) with trusted-proxy authentication."
  },
  {
    "checkId": "core/doctor/security",
    "severity": "warning",
    "message": "Ensure only configured trusted proxies can reach the Gateway port; block direct clients at a host firewall, network firewall, or loopback bind."
  },
  {
    "checkId": "core/doctor/security",
    "severity": "warning",
    "message": "Docs: https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/trusted-proxy-auth"
  }
]
```

- Observed result after fix: The trusted-proxy Gateway config is no longer reported as `CRITICAL` or "without authentication"; doctor keeps a network-exposure warning with trusted-proxy-specific guidance.
- What was not tested: I did not test a live remote reverse-proxy login flow from this branch; the proof is limited to the doctor/security classification and remediation text.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-security.test.ts` - 40 tests passed
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/doctor-security.ts src/commands/doctor-security.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `npm_config_cache=/private/tmp/openclaw-npm-cache npm exec --yes pnpm@11.2.2 -- lint`

target.title: fix(build): fall back to tsx for build TypeScript scripts
target.body:
This PR was triggered by @smoe hitting a real build failure on a Debian trixie host while moving beyond the packaged OpenClaw install and toward building OpenClaw directly from the git checkout. That host was running Debian’s Node.js 24 build, where `process.features.typescript` was unavailable/false, so build-all’s `node --experimental-strip-types ... .ts` step failed late in the build.

The fix keeps OpenClaw’s existing native TypeScript stripping path for Node builds that support it, but falls back to `tsx` for build-time TypeScript scripts on runners where Node lacks that feature. In other words: the checkout build now works on the affected Debian environment without changing the runtime service install or requiring Debian’s `node-typescript` package.

## Summary

- Fixes `scripts/build-all.mjs` on Node builds where native TypeScript stripping is unavailable even though the build contains TypeScript script steps that expect `--experimental-strip-types` to work.
- When a build-all node step invokes `node --experimental-strip-types <script>.ts`, the runner now checks `process.features?.typescript`; if native stripping is unavailable, it runs the step as `node --import tsx <script>.ts` instead.
- This matters because distro/custom/CI Node binaries can lack native TypeScript stripping support; in that environment the late `write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts` build step fails after earlier build work has already completed.
- The intended outcome is that OpenClaw keeps the native-strip path where Node supports it, while remaining buildable on Node runners without that feature.
- Out of scope: changing the live OpenClaw service install path, changing TypeScript compiler/package dependencies, changing package-manager setup, or replacing build-all’s wider orchestration.
- Success looks like `pnpm build` completing on a real OpenClaw checkout with `process.features.typescript === false`, while native-TypeScript Node environments keep their current arguments.
- Reviewers should focus on the fallback contract, the narrow trigger conditions, and the table-driven regression coverage for fallback, passthrough, extra args, and native-strip preservation.

## Linked context

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- Requested by an operator of an affected OpenClaw installation after a real build failed on their host.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `pnpm build` failed late in `scripts/build-all.mjs` when the `write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts` step attempted to run a TypeScript script through `node --experimental-strip-types` on a Node binary without native TypeScript stripping support.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw checkout on `gentle.functional.domains`, user `clawbio`, Node `v24.15.0`, with `process.features.typescript === false`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node -p "process.version + ' typescript=' + process.features.typescript"`
  - `pnpm test test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`
- Evidence after fix:
  - Environment check output: `v24.15.0 typescript=false`
  - Current focused test result after addressing review feedback: `Test Files 1 passed (1)`, `Tests 39 passed (39)`
  - Full build result from the original patch session: `pnpm build` completed past the previously failing TypeScript-script step.
- Observed result after fix: the affected TypeScript script step is routed through `node --import tsx scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` when native TypeScript stripping is unavailable, while `--experimental-strip-types` is preserved when Node reports TypeScript support.
- What was not tested: switching the live systemd OpenClaw gateway service to this checkout; the running service remained the existing npm-global OpenClaw install.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: proof is from one real Linux/OpenClaw host whose Node binary reports `process.features.typescript=false`; native TypeScript stripping environments are covered by deterministic regression tests rather than a second real host.
- Before evidence: before the patch, the same environment reported `process.features.typescript=false`, and `pnpm build` failed at the late `node --experimental-strip-types scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` build step.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node -p "process.version + ' typescript=' + process.features.typescript"`
- `/home/clawbio/.npm-global/bin/pnpm test test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`
- `pnpm build` during the original patch verification session

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added table-driven coverage for TypeScript node build steps being rewritten to `--import tsx` when native TypeScript stripping is unavailable.
- Added coverage that extra script arguments are forwarded through the `tsx` fallback.
- Added passthrough coverage when args do not start with `--experimental-strip-types`.
- Added passthrough coverage when the strip-types target is not a `.ts` file.
- Added coverage that native TypeScript stripping is preserved when the Node binary supports it.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- `pnpm build` failed late in build-all when running `scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` through `node --experimental-strip-types` on a Node binary with `process.features.typescript=false`.

If no test was added, why not?

- A focused regression test was added in `test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No for runtime users; yes only for build behavior on Node binaries without native TypeScript stripping.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Build command selection for TypeScript scripts in build-all.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The fallback is narrow: it only applies when the step begins with `--experimental-strip-types`, the target is a `.ts` script, and the current Node binary does not report TypeScript support. Existing native-strip behavior is preserved when `process.features.typescript` is available. The compatibility constraint is documented inline above the helper, and regression tests cover fallback, passthrough, extra args, and native-strip preservation.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review of the narrow build-all fallback, inline compatibility comment, and regression tests.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Waiting on maintainer review and CI. External real-environment proof from one affected host is included above.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addressed Claude review feedback from @smoe’s local review pass:
  - added the required inline comment explaining the fallback constraint;
  - added passthrough coverage for non-strip args;
  - added passthrough coverage for non-`.ts` targets;
  - added coverage that extra args are forwarded through the `tsx` fallback.

target.title: perf: split subagent registry runtime imports

## Summary

- Split the subagent registry lazy runtime imports across three narrower runtime entries: `context-engine-init.runtime`, `context-engine-registry.runtime`, and `runtime-plugins.runtime`.

target.title: fix(plugin-runtime): restore channel turn alias
target.body:
## Summary

- Restores the deprecated `core.channel.turn.*` runtime surface as an alias of `core.channel.inbound.*` for shipped channel plugins compiled before the inbound rename.
- Keeps the alias as the same object as `inbound`, so there is no second implementation path or drift in production runtime behavior.
- Updates the PluginRuntime type, plugin runtime mock, API baseline hash, and SDK docs to describe the compatibility alias while keeping new plugin guidance on `inbound.*`.
- Intentionally does not change bundled Feishu source, channel logic, config, or package artifacts.

## Linked context

Closes #91122

Related #91122

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No direct maintainer request; this is a focused compatibility fix for the reported shipped plugin runtime break.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Restores `runtime.channel.turn.*` / `core.channel.turn.*` as a deprecated compatibility alias for old channel plugins while preserving `runtime.channel.inbound.*` as the canonical API.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, Node 24-compatible repo toolchain, branch `fix/91122-runtime-channel-turn-alias` after this patch.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built the runtime, then ran this Node probe against the generated `dist/runtime-channel-UUbgZd8n.js` chunk:

```console
$ node --input-type=module - <<'NODE'
import { createRuntimeChannel } from './dist/runtime-channel-UUbgZd8n.js';
const channel = createRuntimeChannel({
  providerControl: {},
  createUnifiedProviderSetup: () => ({}),
  ensureRuntimeAuthDeps: () => ({}),
  runtimeAdapters: {},
  createAgentStateDB: () => ({}),
  createAgentStateDBForStorePath: () => ({}),
  registerInboundSession: () => {},
  runWithDispatch: () => {},
});
console.log(JSON.stringify({
  hasTurn: Boolean(channel.turn),
  sameObject: channel.turn === channel.inbound,
  sameRun: channel.turn.run === channel.inbound.run,
  sameBuildContext: channel.turn.buildContext === channel.inbound.buildContext,
  sameDispatchReply: channel.turn.dispatchReply === channel.inbound.dispatchReply,
  sameRunPreparedReply: channel.turn.runPreparedReply === channel.inbound.runPreparedReply,
}));
NODE
```

Evidence after fix:

```console
{"hasTurn":true,"sameObject":true,"sameRun":true,"sameBuildContext":true,"sameDispatchReply":true,"sameRunPreparedReply":true}
```

Observed result after fix: The built runtime exposes `channel.turn`; `channel.turn === channel.inbound`; all four legacy functions are the same function references as the canonical inbound runtime helpers.

What was not tested: I did not install `openclaw@2026.6.x` with `@openclaw/feishu@2026.5.28` or run a live Feishu DM against real credentials.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof validates the exact built runtime compatibility surface that old compiled plugins dereference. It does not prove a full Feishu WebSocket setup because that requires live Feishu credentials and an older published plugin install.

Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Issue #91122 reports old plugins failing on `core.channel.turn.run()` because the runtime only exposed `channel.inbound.*`.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/runtime/runtime-channel.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache-91122 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.test.ts`

I ran the final UI build stage separately with `node scripts/ui.js build`, which passed. An earlier full `pnpm build` completed successfully on this worktree before the final test-helper alias correction.
