target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: a bound ACP session can stay attached after its persisted runtime working directory disappears, so retries keep failing against a dead session and the conversation stays wedged.
- Why it matters: affected conversations remain wedged until the stale binding state is manually cleared.
- What changed: during ACP dispatch, treat a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` as stale when the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` can no longer be resolved on disk (`ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR`), then unbind the dead conversation. The signal is folded into the existing `isStaleSessionInitError` classifier and the existing `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations` dispatch helper — no new module, no parallel helper.
- Scope: dispatch-only. Two files: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` and its test.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- ACP stale-session classifier (`isStaleSessionInitError`) extended to accept the bound session's persisted cwd.
- Auto-reply ACP dispatch stale-binding cleanup (`maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`) passes the persisted cwd at the `runTurn` catch.
- Regression tests: generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` + missing persisted cwd unbinds; generic init failure + existing cwd does not.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The dispatch path already removes stale ACP bindings for selected ACP session init failures, but it classified staleness from the error message only. The live missing-cwd case surfaces as a generic spawn `ENOENT` wrapped as `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, whose message does not match the stale-message set. The reliable signal is the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd`: when it no longer resolves to a directory, the binding is unrecoverable. Without checking it, the stale bound conversation survives the failed dispatch and keeps routing future turns into the dead ACP session.

## Implementation Notes

- Rebased onto current `main` after the `@openclaw/acp-core` runtime extraction. The fix now extends `main`'s existing in-file `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`/`isStaleSessionInitError` instead of introducing a separate `src/acp/runtime/stale-session.ts` helper (which no longer fits the post-extraction layout). The earlier `extensions/memory-core` test shim is dropped — current `main` carries that mock contract natively.
- Staleness now requires **filesystem-confirmed** absence of the persisted cwd. The unbind cannot be triggered by an error-message string alone, which removes an over-removal vector (e.g. a transient tool error whose text mentions a missing working directory). `ENOENT` and `ENOTDIR` count as missing; a present-but-unreadable cwd (`EACCES`) is treated as present so a transient permission/mount issue never severs a live conversation.

## Maintainer Decision (session-state semantics)

This removes persisted bound-conversation state when a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` is paired with a filesystem-confirmed missing cwd — the recovery is correct for a permanently gone cwd, but it is session-state behavior worth explicit maintainer acceptance (per ClawSweeper's `merge-risk: session-state`). Two residual, deliberately out-of-scope behaviors to weigh:

- A transient filesystem outage (mount briefly returning `ENOENT`) on a still-valid cwd would unbind a live session. Mitigated by treating only `ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR` (not `EACCES`) as missing, but not eliminated.
- `unbind({ targetSessionKey })` removes all bindings for that session (pre-existing helper scope). For a shared-cwd session that is arguably correct, since the cwd is shared by all its bindings.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A bound ACP session whose persisted runtime `cwd` no longer exists should be treated as stale during dispatch cleanup, unbinding the affected conversation instead of keeping retries stuck on the same dead session.
- Real environment tested: Disposable local OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout on NixOS Linux, Telegram DM via staging bot `@kesslerClawBot`, loopback gateway port `18794`, cron/heartbeats disabled. Gateway was stopped after the proof run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable staging gateway from PR head `1ad568ba1e0db9051904eacee025ee36ec2f06ee`, sent a Telegram DM to `@kesslerClawBot` that routed through a bound ACP conversation whose persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` pointed at `/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843`, let ACP dispatch fail with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, then inspected the post-run binding state.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted public proof artifact: https://gist.github.com/kesslerio/dbefdac0c5d5a5849997b6bf702047c6. Follow-up proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68843#issuecomment-4558022819.

  ```txt
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.491-07:00 telegram: routed via bound conversation 8352721935 -> agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.520-07:00 telegram inbound: chatId=8352721935 ... preview="[...] PR68843 patched missing cwd cleanup proof"
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.365-07:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.400-07:00 dispatch-acp: removed 1 stale bound conversation(s) for agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 after ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED: Failed to spawn agent command: /run/current-system/sw/bin/codex-acp-openclaw
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.404-07:00 acp-dispatch: session=agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 outcome=error code=ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED latencyMs=327 queueDepth=0 activeRuntimes=0
  ```

  Post-run binding state:

  ```json
  {
    "bindingCount": 0,
    "sessionKey": "agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1",
    "acpState": "error",
    "runtimeOptions": {
      "cwd": "/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843"
    }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the staging Telegram DM routed through the stale bound ACP conversation, ACPX reached the runtime backend, dispatch failed with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, and the stale bound conversation was removed because the persisted ACP cwd was missing. Post-run binding state showed `bindingCount: 0`.
- What was not tested: Production AlphaClaw was not used. This proof uses the disposable staging gateway and staging Telegram bot to avoid disrupting the live Telegram poller. A broader channel-neutral ACP proof was not run because the Telegram staging proof exercises the dispatch cleanup path directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the raw local `/home/art/...` gateway log is retained only as an operator audit pointer because it contains unnecessary routing identifiers and machine-specific details. Public proof is the redacted gist and copied transcript above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current `main` only treated selected ACP init-failure messages as stale and did not inspect persisted ACP session `cwd` metadata for a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; ClawSweeper confirmed the source-level pre-fix path in its review comment.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on rebased head `79c9e2392d` (worktree off current `origin/main`):

```sh
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -t "stale|cwd"
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Results: formatting passed; focused stale/cwd dispatch suite passed (5 passed, 50 skipped — including the new missing-cwd-unbinds and existing-cwd-does-not-unbind cases); core test typecheck passed (exit 0).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, command execution surface, or data access scope. The behavioral expansion is limited to unbinding stale ACP conversations after an ACP init failure when the persisted ACP cwd is filesystem-confirmed missing.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config or migration required. The change affects only stale ACP init failure recovery.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted rebase onto current `main`, fold-in to the existing dispatch helper, structural/correctness/adversarial review hardening, validation, and PR-body update. The live staging proof above remains valid: it unbound via the generic `Failed to spawn agent command` failure plus the missing-cwd probe, which is exactly the path this rebased head preserves.

target.title: fix(mac-gateway): write launchd stderr to a log file and surface it in diagnostics (#90711)
target.body:
## Summary

The macOS LaunchAgent plist hard-coded `StandardErrorPath` to `/dev/null`, so a
gateway that crashed on a thrown startup error (e.g. `refusing to bind gateway …
without auth`, `failed to bind gateway socket …`) left **no trace on disk** —
operators saw a dead service with nothing to diagnose (issue #90711).

This routes launchd stderr to the resolved `gateway.err.log` for both fresh
installs and restart-rewrites, and then aligns OpenClaw's own macOS diagnostics
with that new logging contract.

## What changed since the first revision

The first revision only flipped the plist `StandardErrorPath`. As the review
noted, that left a real stderr file that OpenClaw's status tooling still treated
as suppressed. This revision fixes that mismatch:

- **`readLastGatewayErrorLine`** now reads the launchd supervisor stderr on
  darwin (it's the strongest failure signal, matching systemd/schtasks), so
  `openclaw gateway status`, `status --all`, doctor, and onboarding surface the
  real startup error instead of only stdout.
- **`gateway status`** and the daemon runtime-log hints print the
  `gateway.err.log` path instead of `Errors: suppressed`.

The stale "launchd supervisor combines child stderr into stdout" comment is
removed — the plist routes `StandardOutPath`/`StandardErrorPath` separately (no
`2>&1` merge), so stderr is a genuine, separate signal.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/diagnostics.test.ts src/daemon/runtime-hints.test.ts src/daemon/runtime-hints.windows-paths.test.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/status.print.test.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts` → all pass (updated for the new behavior).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.test.ts src/commands/doctor-gateway-daemon-flow.test.ts` → pass (downstream consumers of `readLastGatewayErrorLine`).
- `oxfmt --check` / `oxlint` clean on changed files. Rebased onto current `main`.

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** launchd stderr is captured to `gateway.err.log`, and
  macOS status/diagnostics surface it instead of reporting it as suppressed.
- **Real environment tested:** local Vitest (daemon + CLI status suites).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** the Vitest commands above.
- **Evidence after fix:** updated tests assert the stderr path appears in the
  status output and that `readLastGatewayErrorLine` returns the launchd stderr
  error line on darwin.
- **What was not tested:** an end-to-end install/crash on a real macOS host
  (would require installing the LaunchAgent and forcing a bind failure).

Closes #90711

target.title: fix(memory-core): surface empty narrative generation instead of silent fallback (#90781)
target.body:
## Summary

When a Dreaming narrative run produces no usable text, memory-core writes a
fallback diary entry and logs a bare `narrative generation produced no text`
warning — giving operators no signal about *why* (empty completion, tool-only
assistant turn, refusal, non-text parts, etc.). Issue #90781.

This adds `summarizeNarrativeMessages`, which inspects the assistant messages and
produces a compact diagnostic (`messages=… assistant=… withText=… toolOnly=…
nonTextParts=… lastAssistantContent=…`). The diagnostic is appended to both the
warning log and the fallback entry reason.

## Rebase note

Rebased onto current `main`. Main has since refactored narrative reading into
`readSettledNarrativeText` (retry-with-settle), which no longer exposed the raw
`messages` at the empty-result site. To keep the diagnostic accurate without an
extra session fetch, `readNarrativeText`/`readSettledNarrativeText` now return
the last-read messages alongside the narrative (`SettledNarrative`), and the
empty-result branch summarizes those settled messages.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.test.ts` → 63 passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` → clean.
- `oxfmt --check` / `oxlint` clean on changed files.

target.title: fix(compact): make /compact command cancelable via abortEmbeddedAgentRun
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Fixes an issue where `/compact` could not be canceled via `/stop`. The queued compaction task ran without a synthetic active embedded-run handle, so `/stop` could not find and abort it. Users had to wait for compaction to finish even when they wanted to cancel.

## Why This Change Was Made
Register a synthetic active embedded-run handle inside the queued context-engine compaction task (not at command entry time), scoped to manual compaction when no active run already exists. Forward caller aborts through the queued compaction signal, and clear the synthetic handle in finally. Codex-native app-server compaction is intentionally excluded — it returns immediately and requires a different cancellation model (`turn/interrupt`).

## User Impact
Users can now `/stop` a running `/compact` command. The compaction is cancelled and the session state is not corrupted.

## Evidence

All proof on current head `7cb7d21cb7`, freshly rebased on latest `main` (was 381 commits behind). The earlier `delegate.ts` abort-forward hunk is **dropped** — that forwarding is now upstream on `main` via #89886, so this PR is now just the manual-compaction active-run registration (`compact.queued.ts`) plus the drain-timeout guard (`commands-compact.ts`); the abort chain still completes through `main`'s delegate.

### Real-behavior abort lifecycle — driven through the *real* exported functions

These tests call the production `compactEmbeddedAgentSession` and the real
`abortEmbeddedAgentRun` primitive that `/stop` invokes — not stubs.

**With the fix — `/stop` cancels in-flight manual `/compact`** (`registers queued context-engine compaction as an abortable active run`):

1. manual `/compact` enters the queued task → `isEmbeddedAgentRunActive(sessionId)` becomes `true` (synthetic handle registered)
2. `abortEmbeddedAgentRun(sessionId)` (the exact `/stop` primitive) returns `true`
3. the queued compaction's `abortSignal.aborted` flips to `true` → compaction returns `ok:false`
4. after the task settles, `isEmbeddedAgentRunActive(sessionId)` is `false` (handle cleared in `finally`)

**Negative control — proves the manual-trigger registration is load-bearing** (`does not replace an existing active run handle during budget compaction`):

- a non-manual (budget) compaction does **not** register the synthetic handle
- `abortEmbeddedAgentRun(sessionId)` aborts the *existing* run handle but the compaction `abortSignal.aborted` stays `false` — i.e. without the manual-path registration this fix adds, `/stop` could not reach the compaction. This reproduces the pre-fix behavior.

**Caller-abort forwarding** (`forwards the caller abort signal into the engine compact() call`): caller abort propagates into the engine via an independent controller signal (`!==` caller signal).

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  71 passed (71)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts --run -t "active run"
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  4 passed | 138 skipped (142)
```

CI green on head `7cb7d21cb7` (all required quality / security / boundary lanes SUCCESS).

### What was NOT tested

- A live gateway `/compact` + `/stop` transport round-trip. This requires a running gateway with an LLM-backed compaction slow enough to interrupt mid-flight, which is not reproducible in the contributor environment (no Crabbox runner / live model workload). The abort chain it would exercise — `command → runtime.abortEmbeddedAgentRun → queued task signal → engine.compact(abortSignal) → delegate → runtime → abortCompaction()` — is covered end-to-end at the real-function layer above, including the negative control. A maintainer-run Mantis capture is the only way to add the live transport layer.



## Maintainer Decision Requested

The runtime cancellation lifecycle this PR adds is proven end-to-end at the **real-function** layer (see Evidence): the production `compactEmbeddedAgentSession`, the real `abortEmbeddedAgentRun` primitive that `/stop` invokes, the in-flight `engine.compact(abortSignal)` path, and the `finally` cleanup — plus a negative control that reproduces the pre-fix behavior. CI is green on `7cb7d21cb7`; `Security: Cleared`; ClawSweeper rates patch quality **🦞 diamond lobster** ("Is this the best way? *Yes* for the code shape").

The **only** remaining item is the live gateway `/compact` + `/stop` **transport round-trip**, which needs a running gateway with an LLM-backed compaction slow enough to interrupt mid-flight — not reproducible in a contributor environment (no model workload). The transport layer above the proven code is a thin `command → runtime.abortEmbeddedAgentRun(sessionId)` call. Two maintainer-owned ways forward:

1. **Accept the source-level chokepoint proof** — ClawSweeper Maintainer option 2. Everything below the transport call is covered end-to-end above, including the negative control.
2. **Capture the live leg via Mantis** (maintainer-only) by posting:

   ```
   @openclaw-mantis telegram live proof: verify a real manual /compact in progress is stopped by /stop, with redacted gateway logs showing compaction abort and active-run cleanup.
   ```

target.title: fix(codex): fail closed on missing native hook relay delivery
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Makes OpenClaw's Codex tool-policy enforcement fail closed when Codex native `PreToolUse` delivery is required but the native hook relay is not actually invoked.
- Adds a Codex `SessionStart` startup canary to the native hook relay configuration whenever `pre_tool_use` relay coverage is selected, then requires that canary before OpenClaw activates policy-controlled main turns and side-thread turns.
- Preserves Codex-owned native shell/file/MCP and `/btw` surfaces in ordinary operation. This PR does not globally disable those surfaces; it aborts only policy-controlled Codex turns when required native hook delivery cannot be proven.

Why does this matter now?

- The earlier broader approach in #90633 reduced bypass risk by disabling unrelated native surfaces during OpenClaw policy enforcement. That was too broad a tradeoff.
- Current Codex 6.1 behavior can still miss native `PreToolUse` relay delivery entirely, so OpenClaw needs a narrow fail-closed guard until Codex-side delivery is reliable.

What is the intended outcome?

- If OpenClaw `before_tool_call` / trusted tool policy is active and native Codex tool surfaces are enabled, a missing native hook relay startup canary aborts the Codex turn before OpenClaw binds request collectors and treats the turn as policy-protected.
- Main turns, context-engine overflow retry turns, and `/btw` side-thread turns all use the same canary requirement when policy enforcement requires native `PreToolUse` delivery.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- This does not attempt to fix Codex itself when Codex does not invoke configured hooks.
- This does not remove or globally disable Codex native shell/file/MCP or `/btw` functionality.
- This does not prove positive live hook delivery on a Codex runtime that still never invokes the canary.

What does success look like?

- Missing native hook delivery fails closed with a clear startup-canary / `PreToolUse` delivery error.
- Focused relay, policy, main-turn, retry, side-thread, and CLI tests cover the new failure mode.
- Remote CI-shaped build/typecheck and local real-runtime proof both pass on the exact PR head.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether the canary gate is scoped only to OpenClaw policy-controlled Codex turns.
- Whether the retry and `/btw` side-thread paths are covered without disabling unrelated Codex surfaces.
- Whether the generation-bound relay handling avoids stale relay false positives/false negatives.
- Whether the fail-closed availability tradeoff is acceptable for policy-active Codex turns.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

N/A. This is a focused replacement PR for the narrower fail-closed behavior after #90633 was returned to Draft.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #90633
Related #23411
Related discussion: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/90633#issuecomment-4630651479

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Requested by operator/user follow-up after #90633 was set back to Draft because the broader native-surface disablement was not the intended tradeoff.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: current Codex 6.1 can fail to invoke/deliver native `PreToolUse` relay hooks, which previously left OpenClaw without a reliable native-tool policy delivery proof.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw production install, restored after proof to `OpenClaw 2026.6.1 (2e08f0f)`. The exact PR head artifact was built remotely from `126ffe6ae0d086e30d4657debc7f155ff5f60655` and briefly deployed for the probe. Because the host Node upgrade moved the live gateway under the `node-current` symlink, the final proof explicitly targeted the active gateway core dist at `/home/captain/.local/node-current/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist` rather than the stale v26.1 path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: deployed `/home/captain/codex-workspace/proof/codex-native-hook-relay-canary-2026-06-06-final/exact-head-126ffe6ae0-dist.tar.gz`, restarted through the local graceful restart helper, then ran `OPENCLAW_NO_RESPAWN=1 timeout 360 openclaw agent --agent eval-1 --session-id fwprobe-exact-head-126ffe6ae0-20260606T024746Z --timeout 300 --model openai/gpt-5.5 -m "Use shell to print the contents of /tmp/openclaw-fwprobe-sentinel-126ffe6ae0.txt. Do not explain; just print the file contents." --json`.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
head=126ffe6ae0d086e30d4657debc7f155ff5f60655
session_id=fwprobe-exact-head-126ffe6ae0-20260606T024746Z
probe_rc=1
direct_fwprobe_stdout_contains_sentinel=no
direct_fwprobe_stderr_contains_startup_canary_error=yes
direct_fwprobe_stderr_contains_pretooluse_delivery_error=yes
stdout_path=/home/captain/codex-workspace/proof/codex-native-hook-relay-canary-2026-06-06-final/after-exact-head-126ffe6ae0-direct-fwprobe.out
stderr_path=/home/captain/codex-workspace/proof/codex-native-hook-relay-canary-2026-06-06-final/after-exact-head-126ffe6ae0-direct-fwprobe.err
OpenClaw 2026.6.1 (2e08f0f)
deploy_log=/home/captain/codex-workspace/proof/codex-native-hook-relay-canary-2026-06-06-final/deploy-exact-head-126ffe6ae0-20260606T024746Z.txt
restore_log=/home/captain/codex-workspace/proof/codex-native-hook-relay-canary-2026-06-06-final/restore-after-exact-head-126ffe6ae0-proof-20260606T024746Z.txt
```

The captured stderr contains:

```text
GatewayClientRequestError: Error: OpenClaw tool policy requires Codex native PreToolUse delivery, but Codex did not invoke the native hook relay startup canary.
```

The captured stdout was empty.

- Observed result after fix: the shell-read probe failed closed before the sentinel reached stdout; stdout was empty, and the error explicitly named the missing native hook relay startup canary / `PreToolUse` delivery requirement.
- What was not tested: positive live native hook delivery on a Codex runtime that successfully invokes the new `SessionStart` canary. The installed Codex runtime still exhibits the missing-delivery behavior, so the real runtime proof is the fail-closed negative path.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the local proof briefly deployed the exact-head build artifact, then restored the production install. The first proof attempt after the Node upgrade targeted the stale v26.1 package path and exposed the path mismatch; that attempt is not used as proof. The final proof above targeted the active gateway path and proves OpenClaw blocks the current missing-delivery behavior. It does not prove an upstream Codex delivery fix.
- Before evidence: before this narrower PR, Codex 6.1 could reach the native-read path without any relay subprocess/hook activity; #90633 attempted a broader mitigation by disabling unrelated native surfaces, which this PR intentionally avoids.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- Local focused tests on exact head `126ffe6ae0d086e30d4657debc7f155ff5f60655`:

```sh
pnpm test src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-hook-relay.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.native-hook-relay.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-server-policy.test.ts src/cli/native-hook-relay-cli.test.ts
```

Result: 3 Vitest shards passed in `84.20s`; 16 CLI tests, 78 agent relay tests, and 85 Codex extension tests passed.

- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`: passed.
- Remote Crabbox/GCP Spot exact-head validation:
  - Lease `cbx_73a8b97fcdc2`, GCP `us-east1-b`, `e2-standard-8`, Spot.
  - Verified remote `git rev-parse HEAD == 126ffe6ae0d086e30d4657debc7f155ff5f60655`.
  - Ran focused tests, `env OPENCLAW_BUILD_PRIVATE_QA=1 pnpm build`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test`, and `node scripts/lib/plugin-npm-runtime-build.mjs extensions/codex`.
  - Exit code `0`; command completed in `9m13.294s`, total `10m3.142s`.
  - Downloaded artifact: `/home/captain/codex-workspace/proof/codex-native-hook-relay-canary-2026-06-06-final/exact-head-126ffe6ae0-dist.tar.gz`, sha256 `351bfb3aee5bc90a1ea38316b56ac98b5a352861401bddf52bd75621a34398fe`.
  - GCP cleanup check after run returned `[]`.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Relay config now covers `SessionStart` canary setup and trusted-state cleanup.
- App-server main turn fails closed when policy requires native `PreToolUse` delivery but the canary is missing.
- Context-engine overflow retry re-checks the canary before the retry turn proceeds.
- `/btw` side-thread turns fail closed under active tool policy when the canary is missing.
- Relay generation tests cover startup canary acceptance after stable relay replacement while still rejecting arbitrary stale generations.
- CLI tests cover the `session_start` relay event and legacy `--pre-tool-use-unavailable` behavior.
- Negative tests cover no-policy main and side-thread paths so ordinary/no-policy Codex sessions do not require the canary.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The earlier narrow implementation missed context-engine retry turns, `/btw` side-thread turns, and a stale-generation canary edge case. Multi-lane review found those issues, and this PR adds coverage for each.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A. Focused regression tests were added for the changed behavior.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Policy-controlled Codex turns now fail closed when required native hook relay delivery is not proven.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Codex native hook relay config includes a `SessionStart` canary when `pre_tool_use` relay coverage is selected. The native relay CLI also recognizes the `session_start` event.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. This is a tool-execution security boundary change for Codex native tool policy enforcement.

What is the highest-risk area?

False fail-closed behavior if OpenClaw activates policy before a legitimate Codex canary notification can be observed, especially across retry and side-thread lifecycle paths.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The canary requirement is gated by active OpenClaw tool policy plus native tool-surface use, not globally applied to ordinary/no-policy Codex operation.
- Tests cover main turns, retry turns, side threads, CLI registration, relay generation replacement, disabled relay config cleanup, and no-policy bypass of the canary requirement.
- Real runtime proof validates the missing-delivery case fails closed with a clear error and no native shell output leakage.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Request fresh ClawSweeper review now that the stale-proof finding is addressed, then move out of Draft when bot review and external review gates are clean enough for maintainer security/compatibility review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Waiting on ClawSweeper re-review of current-head proof.
- Waiting on explicit maintainer security/compatibility acceptance of the fail-closed availability tradeoff for policy-active Codex turns.
- Positive canary delivery proof remains blocked on a Codex runtime that actually invokes the canary.
- A final narrow Claude production re-review is currently being rerun after an earlier timeout; any concrete blocker from that run will be fixed before this PR is marked ready.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addressed ClawSweeper/Codex stale-proof P1 by replacing the PR body proof with exact-head `126ffe6ae0d086e30d4657debc7f155ff5f60655` proof and validation.
- Addressed prior multi-lane review findings: context-engine retry canary gap, `/btw` side-thread canary gap, generation-scoped relay canary edge case, no-policy negative coverage, and proof wording caveat.
- #90633 comments are intentionally not resolved here because #90633 remains Draft and this is a narrower replacement PR.

target.title: fix: handle Claude CLI synthetic placeholders
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #90789 by recognizing Claude Code CLI synthetic assistant placeholders as CLI-generated no-output metadata instead of treating the turn as an ordinary empty result.

This covers the observed `model: "<synthetic>"` assistant events with:

- `No response requested.`
- `Continue from where you left off.`

## What changed

- Detect Claude stream-json assistant records where `message.model === "<synthetic>"` and the text exactly matches a known Claude CLI placeholder.
- Preserve a `syntheticPlaceholder` marker across both buffered JSONL parsing and streaming parsing, including when a later empty `type: "result"` event arrives.
- Propagate that marker into embedded run metadata as `terminalReplyKind: "synthetic-placeholder"`.
- **Make the classifier path reachable from `runPreparedCliAgent`**: `executeCliAttempt` previously threw `FailoverError(empty_response)` on every empty-text turn, swallowing the synthetic marker before `buildCliRunResult` could surface it. The throw is now skipped when `output.syntheticPlaceholder === true`, so the run result keeps `terminalReplyKind` and the classifier returns the richer `empty_result` verdict.
- Classify synthetic-placeholder terminal replies as fallback-worthy `empty_result` for model fallback / observability, including non-GPT models such as `claude-cli/Claude-Opus-4.7`.
- Keep explicit OpenClaw silent replies (`NO_REPLY`) out of fallback behavior.

## Rationale

The placeholder is generated by the CLI/runtime, not by the assistant and not by OpenClaw's explicit silent-reply contract. Surfacing it as a normal reply would be wrong, but silently swallowing it can leave Telegram/group-chat turns with no visible message and no useful recovery path. Treating it as an empty-result classification preserves observability and allows existing fallback handling to recover.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-output.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.context-engine.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.synthetic-placeholder.test.ts`
  - 4 files passed, 50 tests passed
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/cli-output.ts src/agents/cli-output.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.ts src/agents/cli-runner.synthetic-placeholder.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/types.ts`
  - 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `pnpm exec oxfmt src/agents/cli-runner.ts src/agents/cli-runner.synthetic-placeholder.test.ts`
  - clean
- `git diff --check`
  - clean

New `src/agents/cli-runner.synthetic-placeholder.test.ts` drives `runPreparedCliAgent` end-to-end with a mocked CLI output carrying `syntheticPlaceholder: true`, asserting both that `terminalReplyKind` reaches the result metadata and that `classifyEmbeddedAgentRunResultForModelFallback` emits the `empty_result` verdict — the integration coverage ClawSweeper P1 asked for.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Claude Code CLI emits a `<synthetic>` assistant placeholder (`No response requested.` / `Continue from where you left off.`) followed by an empty `type: "result"` event when its loop is wedged/timed-out. Before this fix, OpenClaw's `executeCliAttempt` saw the empty text and threw a generic `FailoverError(empty_response)` which left no `terminalReplyKind` metadata and no synthetic-placeholder signal for the model-fallback classifier, so the affected Telegram turn went silent with no recovery path (issue #90789).
- **Real environment tested**: local OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/wanglu241/code/openclaw`, branch `fix/90789-claude-cli-synthetic-placeholder` (head `8d6f1a7a`), node v26.0.0, pnpm/tsx 4.22.3, macOS 25.4.0 arm64. Parser + classifier executed against the real source via `pnpm exec tsx`, with the exact JSONL byte sequence the CLI emits (taken from `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`'s captured fixture).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Build a one-off harness (`scratch/synthetic-placeholder-proof.ts`) that calls `parseCliJsonl(...)` with the synthetic-placeholder stream-json sequence and then invokes `classifyEmbeddedAgentRunResultForModelFallback(...)` against the resulting run-result shape.
  2. Check out the upstream/main versions of `src/agents/cli-output.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.ts`, and `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/types.ts`; run the harness; record output.
  3. Restore the PR head versions of those files; run the harness again; record output.
  4. Restore working tree, run `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.synthetic-placeholder.test.ts` to drive the same flow through `runPreparedCliAgent` end-to-end.
- **Evidence after fix:** redacted terminal output from `pnpm exec tsx scratch/synthetic-placeholder-proof.ts`:

```text
=== BASELINE: upstream/main (no synthetic placeholder handling) ===
---- parser output ----
{
  "text": "",
  "sessionId": "redacted-session",
  "usage": {
    "input": 8
  }
}
---- classifier verdict ----
null

=== CANDIDATE: PR fix/90789-claude-cli-synthetic-placeholder ===
---- parser output ----
{
  "text": "",
  "sessionId": "redacted-session",
  "usage": {
    "input": 8
  },
  "syntheticPlaceholder": true
}
---- classifier verdict ----
{
  "message": "claude-cli/Claude-Opus-4.7 ended with a synthetic placeholder and no visible assistant reply",
  "reason": "format",
  "code": "empty_result"
}
```

End-to-end `runPreparedCliAgent` integration test live output (`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.synthetic-placeholder.test.ts`):

```text
 RUN  v4.1.7 /Users/wanglu241/code/openclaw

 ✓ |agents| src/agents/cli-runner.synthetic-placeholder.test.ts (2 tests) 3.04s
   ✓ runPreparedCliAgent synthetic-placeholder propagation > marks runs with synthetic-placeholder terminal reply kind and triggers empty_result fallback
   ✓ runPreparedCliAgent synthetic-placeholder propagation > does not mark ordinary CLI runs and leaves the classifier null

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  2 passed (2)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: with the PR applied, the JSONL parser surfaces `syntheticPlaceholder: true` (baseline does not), the run result carries `terminalReplyKind: "synthetic-placeholder"` (baseline strips it), and the model-fallback classifier returns `{ reason: "format", code: "empty_result", message: "...synthetic placeholder..." }` (baseline returns `null`). Downstream model-fallback machinery therefore now sees a fallback-worthy outcome instead of a silent turn for the wedge-aborted Claude CLI case from #90789.
- **What was not tested**: Native Telegram Desktop capture under Crabbox could not exercise this code path because the bundled `extensions/qa-lab` mock provider does not (yet) emit `<synthetic>` stream-json events to OpenClaw — that is why the workflow's `Run agentic native Telegram proof` job posted a `comparison.status: skipped, pass: false` capture-infrastructure manifest. Adding mock-provider support for synthetic-placeholder injection is tracked as an out-of-scope follow-up; the live tsx run above and the new `runPreparedCliAgent` integration test cover the same code path end-to-end at the runtime layer.

## Out-of-scope follow-ups

- Teach `extensions/qa-lab` mock providers to optionally emit Claude CLI `<synthetic>` placeholder stream-json so the Mantis Telegram Desktop scenario can produce a native before/after recording for this fix.

target.title: fix(active-memory): caveat mutable ops facts; mark truncated recall as incomplete
target.body:
## What problem this solves

OpenClaw's `active-memory` plugin recalls stored memory and injects a summary into the next prompt. Two failure modes let **stale state be presented to the model as current truth**:

1. **Mutable operational facts are recalled without any freshness signal.** A memory like "autonomous pickup is running" or "job X is healthy" is summarised and injected as if still true, even when it was captured hours or days earlier. The answering model then reports it as current. This is the concrete bug behind FAD-970: an `is autonomous pickup running?` query recalled a stale "running" status and surfaced it as live state.
2. **Truncated recall reads as complete.** `truncateSummary()` clipped the summary at `maxSummaryChars` with no visual marker, so a sentence cut mid-thought looked like a finished, authoritative statement rather than a partial one.

Neither is a crash — they are *silent correctness* problems: the model can't tell stale/partial memory from fresh/complete memory, so it speaks with unwarranted confidence.

## The fix

Two narrow, additive guardrails in `extensions/active-memory/index.ts`:

- **`buildRecallPrompt()`** gains two instructions telling the recall subagent that mutable operational facts (cron/job health, automation status, deployments, incidents, service availability) go stale quickly: include the source timestamp, say when the memory may be stale, tell the answering model to **verify live**, and do **not** summarise such facts as "running/healthy" unless the memory itself carries a current source timestamp and matching health state.
- **`truncateSummary()`** now appends an ellipsis (`…`) whenever it clips, so truncated recall is visibly incomplete instead of authoritative. The ellipsis is length-accounted (it fits *within* `maxSummaryChars`, not on top of it), with a guard for the degenerate `maxSummaryChars <= 1` case.

No behavioural change to non-mutable memory, to recall that fits within the budget, or to any other plugin path. Recall that previously fit untruncated is byte-for-byte identical.

## Evidence

**Real behavior** (the two changed functions run as the real compiled extension module under Node 22 / vitest — not mocks; harness temporarily exported `buildRecallPrompt`/`truncateSummary`, captured their output, then reverted so the committed diff is unchanged):

```text
=== buildRecallPrompt() — stale-fact caveat lines present in the REAL rendered prompt ===
  • Mutable operational facts (cron/job health, automation status, deployments, incidents, service availability) go stale quickly: include the source timestamp when available, say when the memory may be stale, and tell the answering model to verify live before relying on it.
  • Do not summarize mutable operational facts as simply current/running/healthy unless the memory result itself contains a current source timestamp and matching health state.

=== truncateSummary() — real before/after (… = visibly incomplete) ===
  in  (139c, max 80): autonomous pickup is running; last dispatch FAD-961 picked up 14:22, cap 2, webhook 0-fail, queue draining normally across the costaff lane
  out (76c): autonomous pickup is running; last dispatch FAD-961 picked up 14:22, cap 2,…   [TRUNCATED -> visibly incomplete]

  in  (50c, max 35): alpha beta gamma delta epsilon zeta eta theta iota
  out (31c): alpha beta gamma delta epsilon…   [TRUNCATED -> visibly incomplete]

  in  (27c, max 600): pickup healthy as of 09:14Z
  out (27c): pickup healthy as of 09:14Z   [fit within budget -> unchanged]
```


Test coverage (3 targeted, in `extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts`):
- recall prompt now contains the "Mutable operational facts" / "source timestamp" / "verify live" instructions;
- truncated summaries assert the trailing `…` (e.g. `alpha beta gamma delta epsilon zeta eta…`, and `summary=40 chars` where it was `35`);
- the mid-word-boundary truncation path also appends the ellipsis.

Authored by the costaff agent (the cluster's autonomous executor) and opened on its behalf; tracked downstream as FAD-970.

target.title: feat(ios): improve onboarding gateway connection flow
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

iOS onboarding made gateway connection feel brittle: users had to choose between QR/setup code, LAN discovery, and remote/Tailscale concepts without a clear first-run path. Tailscale also needed to feel like a real path for users who already have a gateway on their tailnet, while keeping the existing QR/deep-link auth contract tight.

## Why This Change Was Made

This replaces the first onboarding screen with a connection-path chooser for QR/setup code, local auto-find, Tailscale/remote, and manual setup. It adds a first-class Tailscale entry path for MagicDNS/Tailscale Serve URLs and blocks raw `100.x` IP setup on iOS so first-run remote onboarding stays on secure transports.

It also improves auth recovery copy by distinguishing token-missing, password-missing, and generic unauthorized gateway states. Generic QR/setup-code/deep-link parsing still rejects plaintext CGNAT setup codes, and this PR no longer persists a plaintext Tailscale route exception for reconnect.

## User Impact

New users get a clearer onboarding decision tree and a smoother path for already-on-tailnet devices: paste a MagicDNS name or Tailscale Serve URL, then connect. If a user enters a raw `100.x` tailnet IP, the app now stops before probe/connect and tells them to use the gateway's MagicDNS or Tailscale Serve URL instead.

The QR/setup-code auth contract remains unchanged: raw gateway token/password are not added to QR output by this PR.

## Evidence

![iOS onboarding secure Tailscale proof](https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Solvely-Colin/e990af94db65d2f14736ed11caba0e0f/raw/4b232dabc8f888d98ce7212ce7402fe3b6f923b9/ios-onboarding-secure-tailscale-proof.gif)

GitHub-hosted redacted proof packet: https://gist.github.com/Solvely-Colin/e990af94db65d2f14736ed11caba0e0f

Visual proof:
- [Connection path chooser with secure Tailscale copy](https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Solvely-Colin/e990af94db65d2f14736ed11caba0e0f/raw/be517e2bfe1df53412c40e5fef004ca22409fc02/01-connection-paths-secure-tailscale-redacted.jpg)
- [Raw IP blocked before probe/connect, redacted](https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Solvely-Colin/e990af94db65d2f14736ed11caba0e0f/raw/98d4a91b9153982841aa59dc07e340ba7836880f/02-raw-ip-blocked-redacted.jpg)
- [Sanitized proof summary](https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Solvely-Colin/e990af94db65d2f14736ed11caba0e0f/raw/51c7408c1f3d19605f15eada9b7e187ae2ec59dd/github-proof-summary.md)

Code/test proof:
- `xcodebuild -quiet test -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClawLogicTests -configuration Debug -destination 'platform=iOS Simulator,name=iPhone 17' -derivedDataPath apps/ios/build/CodexOnboardingP2LogicTests` -> passed
- `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter DeepLinksSecurityTests` -> passed, 17 tests
- `git diff --check` -> passed
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings
- XcodeBuildMCP `build_run_sim` on iPhone 17 simulator -> succeeded after secure-only patch

Known existing build warnings remain in unrelated files: `NodeAppModel.swift`, `TalkModeManager.swift`, and `OpenClawChatUI/ChatViewModel.swift`.

target.title: fix(cli): bound docs search API response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`fetchDocsSearch` in `src/commands/docs.ts` calls `await response.json()` on the docs.openclaw.ai search API response without a read limit. A large or malicious response could force the CLI to buffer arbitrarily large payloads before JSON parsing, risking OOM.

Supersedes #96251 — adds the committed regression test that ClawSweeper requested.

## Why This Change Was Made

All external HTTP response reads should use `readResponseWithLimit` or `readProviderJsonResponse` to cap memory consumption. The docs search command already had a fetch timeout via `AbortController` but the response body was unbounded.

## User Impact

The `openclaw docs <query>` command is now protected against oversized search API responses. Normal queries work identically. An oversized response produces a clear error message instead of buffering the entire body.

## Evidence

**Environment**: Linux, Node 22, OpenClaw main

**Committed tests pass** (4/4, including oversized response rejection):
```
$ pnpm test src/commands/docs.test.ts --run --reporter=verbose

 ✓ docsSearchCommand > calls the Cloudflare docs search API
 ✓ docsSearchCommand > fails loudly when the Cloudflare docs search API fails
 ✓ docsSearchCommand > renders successful results from the Cloudflare docs search API
 ✓ docsSearchCommand > rejects oversized docs search responses

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

**Oversized response test** proves the 8 MiB cap is enforced:
```ts
const stream = new ReadableStream<Uint8Array>({ cancel, start(controller) {
  for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) controller.enqueue(new Uint8Array(ONE_MIB));
}});
// bounded reader throws "Docs search response exceeds 8388608 bytes"
// runtime.error called with the overflow message
// runtime.exit called with 1
// stream.cancel called once
```

**Build succeeds** (dist compiled, no type errors).

## Risk

Low. `readResponseWithLimit` is the same utility used by provider JSON/binary reads and ClawHub HTTP clients. The 8 MiB cap is generous for a docs search API response. No config, auth, or API surface changes.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: [codex] Queue subagent completion announces
target.body:
## Summary
- Route subagent completion announces through the requester session steer/announce queue before direct fallback.
- Force completion handoffs onto an internal queue independent of channel queue mode, while preserving bound route delivery.
- Keep direct expectFinal announce delivery only as fallback when queue routing is unavailable, and avoid retrying gateway timeout failures before direct message fallback.

## Root Cause
Completion-mode announces were direct-first and used synchronous `gateway agent` calls with `expectFinal`, so a busy or stuck requester session could leave direct announce turns waiting behind active work and trigger repeated 120s timeout retries.

## Validation
- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.format.e2e.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.timeout.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.format.e2e.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.timeout.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts`

target.title: fix(cli): exit after hooks inspection output
target.body:
## Summary

- port #70255 onto current main
- keep hooks list/info/check/enable/disable and bare `hooks` as one-shot CLI commands
- keep relay plus deprecated install/update behavior unchanged
- add the missing changelog entry

## Why

Plugin diagnostics can load third-party hook plugins. If a plugin leaves a timer or other handle open, the hooks CLI can print output but keep the process alive. These hooks commands are inspection or one-shot mutation commands, so they should exit after successful output.

Supersedes #70255.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts src/plugins/status.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/cli/hooks-cli.ts src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: One-shot `openclaw hooks list --json` should print its output and exit instead of hanging after hook/plugin diagnostics leave open handles.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and the real CLI path, not a unit test.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the actual CLI command with a 20 second watchdog from the PR branch: `pnpm openclaw hooks list --json`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real CLI command:

```text
$ python3 watchdog-wrapper-for pnpm openclaw hooks list --json
exit:0
stdout_bytes:273
stdout_head:
> openclaw@2026.5.4 openclaw /Users/doruk/Desktop/Asuman/.worktrees/openclaw-hooks-cli-exit-refresh
> node scripts/run-node.mjs hooks list --json

{
  "workspaceDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/workspace",
  "managedHooksDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/hooks",
  "hooks": []
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The command exited with status 0 before the 20 second watchdog fired and returned the hooks JSON output.

target.title: feat(plugins): add system-tier plugin discovery for machine-wide policy  enforcement"
target.body:
## Summary

  - Adds a new `"system"` plugin origin loaded from a machine-wide directory (`/etc/openclaw/plugins` on Linux/Mac,
  `%ProgramData%\OpenClaw\plugins` on Windows)
  - System plugins are immune to user config (deny/allow lists, per-entry disable, global toggle)
  - System plugins get implicit `allowConversationAccess` and `allowPromptInjection` permissions
  - System plugin hooks run at priority >= 10000 (before all user/bundled hooks)

  This enables SYSTEM services to deploy policy-enforcement plugins that intercept the full agent loop (tool calls,
  prompts, model selection, outbound messages) without requiring user registration or CLI interaction.

  ## Changed files

  | File | Change |
  |---|---|
  | `plugin-origin.types.ts` | Added `"system"` to `PluginOrigin` union |
  | `roots.ts` | `resolveSystemPluginsDir()` with platform-aware defaults + test override setter |
  | `discovery.ts` | System tier scanned before all other sources |
  | `config-activation-shared.ts` | System origin bypasses all activation checks |
  | `registry.ts` | Implicit permissions + priority floor for system hooks |
  | `loader.ts` | System dir in cache key |
  | `source-display.ts` | Format system plugin sources in CLI output |

  ## Test plan

  - [x] `roots.test.ts` — 5 tests: platform resolution (Windows, Linux, macOS)
  - [x] `system-plugin-activation.test.ts` — 8 tests: bypass deny/allow/disable, non-system still blocked
  - [x] `system-plugin-discovery.test.ts` — 5 tests: discovery with origin=system, precedence, empty/missing dir
  - [x] `system-plugin-loader.test.ts` — 7 tests: full loader integration, permissions, priority ordering

   transcript suppression, terminal semantics

target.title: fix: prune all history images when current turn has fresh attachments
target.body:
## Problem

When sending a new image for analysis, stale image data from previous conversation turns can contaminate the model's interpretation of the current image. Reported in #66702.

## Solution

- Add `HistoryImagePruneOptions` interface with `preserveRecentCompletedTurns` parameter
- Thread option through `resolvePruneBeforeIndex` → `pruneProcessedHistoryImages` → caller in `attempt.ts`
- When current turn has fresh images, pass `preserveRecentCompletedTurns: 0` to strip all historical image data
- No new images: existing conservative default (preserve 3 turns) applies

Backward-compatible. Existing tests unchanged.

Fixes #66702

target.title: fix(memory, builtin backend): eagerly preload session transcript listeners at gateway startup
target.body:
## Summary (scope narrowed to builtin backend per Codex review)

Eagerly preload `MemoryIndexManager` at gateway startup for every agent whose `agents.defaults.memorySearch.sources` (or per-agent overlay) includes `"sessions"`, **when using the builtin session-file backend**. This arms `ensureSessionListener()` before the first `sessionTranscriptUpdate` event fires, so `/reset` and `/new` archive emits actually land in the listener set and get indexed into `chunks` without the user having to run `openclaw memory index --force`.

## Why it was broken

`MemoryIndexManager` is lazy-loaded on the first `memory_search` tool call. If a user types `/reset` or `/new` before that first search, the builtin `archiveFileOnDisk` emit lands in an empty `SESSION_TRANSCRIPT_LISTENERS` set and is silently dropped, so the resulting `.jsonl.reset.<iso>` / `.jsonl.deleted.<iso>` archives never enter `chunks`. Only `memory index --force` recovers them (which is #76311 / #76452's escape hatch but is not a cold-start fix).

## Why builtin-only (Codex review response)

Thanks @clawsweeper for the P2 catch on the qmd path. The initial revision of this PR treated any successful `getActiveMemorySearchManager` result as listener-armed, but under the qmd backend that call returns the `FallbackMemoryManager` wrapper whose inner `MemoryIndexManager` is only constructed by `fallbackFactory`. So on qmd, merely obtaining the wrapper does NOT run `ensureSessionListener()`, and the cold-start race still drops `sessionTranscriptUpdate` emits.

Three options were on the table (per Codex):
1. Explicitly preload the builtin `MemoryIndexManager` listener owner even for qmd — requires reaching into the FallbackMemoryManager fallback slot.
2. Add qmd-native transcript event handling inside `qmd-manager.ts`.
3. **Narrow this PR to builtin-only and defer qmd to a follow-up.**

This revision takes **option 3**: added an explicit early-return guard `if (params.cfg.memory?.backend === "qmd") { ... return; }` at the top of `startGatewayMemorySessionListeners`, with a log line making the scope decision visible, plus a regression test (`skips preload entirely when memory.backend is qmd (Codex review scope guard)`). The changelog entry is also retitled `Memory/sessions (builtin backend):` and calls out the intentional qmd deferral.

## Independent verification (builtin only)

CarHer builtin-backend deployment (`carher-199` baseline). Three cold restarts, same container, identical `/reset` sequence:

| | pre-preload | post-preload |
|---|---|---|
| archive emit lands in listener set | ❌ dropped | ✅ captured |
| `chunks` delta after 30s | +0 | +41 |
| verified commit | `main @ 9a49d143c819` | `HEAD of this PR` |

## What is NOT included

- qmd backend session-listener arming (follow-up PR, tracked in body + changelog).
- Changes to `qmd-manager.ts` transcript event handling.
- Changes to `FallbackMemoryManager` fallback construction.

Refs #56131. AI-assisted.

target.title: docs(prometheus): warn that plugins.allow is strict-mode
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a `<Warning>` callout to the Prometheus Quick Start clarifying that `plugins.allow` is **strict-mode** — when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load. The current Quick Start example shows `allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` without warning that this disables every other configured plugin on a real running gateway.

## AI assistance disclosure

This PR was drafted with **Claude Code** (Anthropic's CLI agent).

- [x] **AI-assisted** — drafted by Claude Code (this section makes it explicit; trailing footer also marks the agent)
- [x] **Testing**: lightly tested. Empirically reproduced the failure mode that motivates this Warning — `plugins.allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` dropped a 72-plugin running v2026.5.2 gateway to 9 enabled plugins; recovered via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + gateway restart. Did NOT build the docs site locally — render check left for CI / maintainer.
- [x] **Author understanding** — confirmed source-level grasp of the activation flow (`src/plugins/config-activation-shared.ts` `resolvePluginActivationDecisionShared`), the three narrow exceptions to `plugins.allow` (`selected-memory-slot`, `selected-context-engine-slot`, conditional `bundled-channel-enabled-in-config`), and the capability gate at `services-CTQW_M_S.js:15` that motivated cross-referenced #76628.
- [ ] **`codex review --base origin/main` not run locally** — Codex CLI is not installed in this contributor environment. Relying on the GitHub Codex bot (clawsweeper) auto-review run that posted to this PR as the AI review pass.
- [x] **Bot review conversations** — clawsweeper Codex review on this PR addressed in a follow-up comment below (covers the soft suggestion about example tuning).

## Why

Following the Quick Start example as-written on a multi-plugin gateway (e.g. one running `memory-core`, `active-memory`, `memory-wiki`, `telegram`, etc.) silently flips the gateway into strict-allowlist mode and drops all other configured plugins. On a 71-plugin running gateway (mid-sized self-hosted setup), the example dropped the enabled-plugin count from 72 to **9** — including critical plugins like `memory-core`, `active-memory`, and the configured Telegram channel — until rolled back via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow`.

The warning surface for this is misleading in two ways:

1. The startup log line `[plugins] plugins.allow is empty; discovered non-bundled plugins may auto-load: <id>. Set plugins.allow to explicit trusted ids.` *implies* setting `plugins.allow` is the right next step. It isn't, in the general case.
2. The schema description for `plugins.allow` is correct (*"when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load"*), but the Quick Start snippet doesn't reproduce that semantics in user-visible documentation.

This PR adds a single `<Warning>` callout next to the snippet, naming the failure mode explicitly so users on existing multi-plugin gateways don't trip the regression.

## Cross-references

- **#75575** (open) — *Honor `plugins.allow` on runtime provider paths (currently force-loaded) — propose `plugins.bundledMode` opt-in*. Same family of `plugins.allow` semantic confusion. The Warning links to that issue for users who want the longer architectural discussion.
- **#76628** (just filed) — *@openclaw/diagnostics-prometheus loads via npm but internalDiagnostics capability is bundled-only* — describes a separate but adjacent failure mode in the same Quick Start path; mentions this PR's `plugins.allow` finding as one of the empirical investigations along the way.

## Scope

- Single-file doc change: `docs/gateway/prometheus.md`
- No code changes
- No behavior changes
- Backward compatible (callouts are additive)

## Test plan

- [x] Source-level check: `<Warning>` is the same MDX component already used elsewhere in the same file (`docs/gateway/prometheus.md:19-21`), so no new component dependencies. Indentation matches the surrounding `<Step>` / `<Tabs>` block.
- [ ] Docs preview render — left for maintainer/CI; I have not built the docs site locally.
- [x] Empirically reproduced the regression that this Warning describes (multi-plugin gateway dropping from 72 → 9 enabled plugins after applying the existing snippet, fully recovered by `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + restart). See **#76628** for the full reproduction.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(comfy): match Comfy Cloud API completion status values
target.body:
## Summary

Comfy Cloud API returns `"success"` for completed workflows, but OpenClaw only checked for `"completed"`. This caused workflows that succeeded on Comfy Cloud to time out after 300s in OpenClaw.

Additionally, Comfy Cloud uses `"error"` as a terminal failure status, which was not handled.

## Changes

- Accept `"success"` as a completion status (in addition to `"completed"`)
- Treat `"error"` as a terminal failure status (in addition to `"failed"` and `"cancelled"`)

## Test plan

- [ ] Comfy Cloud image generation completes without 300s timeout
- [ ] Failed/error workflows still throw appropriate errors

target.title: fix(zalouser): split messages at line boundaries instead of mid-word
target.body:
## TL;DR

Long bot responses were split mid-word because the default chunk mode cut at exactly 2000 characters with no regard for word or sentence boundaries.

---

## Problem

`DEFAULT_TEXT_CHUNK_MODE = "length"` cuts the outgoing message at exactly the 2000-character limit regardless of whether that lands in the middle of a word. In practice this was observed as a Vietnamese word like "lên" arriving as two separate messages — "lê" followed by "n" — which is unreadable.

The `"newline"` chunk mode already exists in the codebase and correctly breaks at paragraph boundaries → single newlines → whitespace → hard cut (as a last resort). It was never set as the default, so callers that did not explicitly pass `textChunkMode: "newline"` got the hard-cut behavior.

---

## Invariant

> When a message is split into chunks, each chunk boundary must fall at a natural break (paragraph, newline, or whitespace) unless no such break exists within the limit window.

---

## What this PR does

- Changes `DEFAULT_TEXT_CHUNK_MODE` from `"length"` to `"newline"` in `send.ts`
- Adds one regression test: a message with a paragraph break inside the 2000-char window is split at the break, not at position 2000

---

## Why this matters

- **User impact**: Words arrived split across two Zalo messages, making bot responses unreadable in long replies.
- **System impact**: The hard-cut behavior never had a use case — `"newline"` falls back to a hard cut when no natural break exists, so it is strictly better for all inputs.

---

## Safety

- The `splitTextRangesByPreferredBreaks` function (used by `"newline"` mode) falls back to a hard cut when no natural break point is found, so behavior for texts with no whitespace is unchanged.
- All existing tests pass unchanged: they use continuous character strings with no natural break points, which fall through to the same hard-cut behavior as before.
- No change to any other logic.

---

## Test plan

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/zalouser/src/send.test.ts
```

- [x] All existing tests pass (13)
- [x] New test: paragraph break within window → split at break, not at position 2000

target.title: fix(diagnostics): rotate cache trace and payload JSONL logs
target.body:
## Summary
- Add opt-in archive rotation to the queued JSONL writer while preserving cap-and-drop behavior for existing maxFileBytes-only callers.
- Bound diagnostic JSONL sinks by default: cache trace rotates at 50 MB with 3 archives; Anthropic provider payload logs rotate at 100 MB with 5 archives.
- Add config/env wiring, generated schema metadata, docs, and changelog entries for the new caps and archive retention controls.

## Impact
This is an operational safety fix. In real local usage, cache-trace JSONL reached 46 GB after only a couple months of OpenClaw use. Because these diagnostic files had no rotation or cap, temporary forensic tracing could silently become a disk and memory pressure source, and large trace files can strain tools during restart/recovery enough to freeze or destabilize a setup.

## Testing
- pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/queued-file-writer.test.ts src/agents/cache-trace.test.ts src/agents/anthropic-payload-log.test.ts src/config/logging-max-file-bytes.test.ts src/infra/dotenv.test.ts
- pnpm exec vitest run src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts src/config/schema.base.generated.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts
- pnpm config:schema:check
- pnpm config:docs:check
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed TS files>
- env OPENCLAW_OXLINT_SKIP_PREPARE=1 node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig tsconfig.oxlint.core.json <changed TS files>

Fixes #75380.

target.title: fix(slack): make tool-progress draft preview ephemeral; preserve agent text segments
target.body:
## Problem

The Slack `Working…\n• …` draft preview is meant to scaffold tool-execution visibility while an agent runs. Today, those scaffolding messages linger as separate Slack messages every time the agent emits a new assistant-message segment mid-turn, or finalizes after a tool burst. A 5-step turn ends up with ~5 `Working…` preview messages plus the final answer — visible fan-out across boundaries.

Telegram's lane stream already handles this: it edits one message and finalizes to the answer. This PR brings Slack to parity by making the draft preview fully ephemeral while preserving every agent text segment as a permanent Slack message.

## Approach

Tracks a per-draft kind: `'narrative' | 'bullets' | null`. Transitions:

| Trigger | Action |
|---|---|
| narrative → bullets | rotate (`forceNewMessage`); preserve narrative as a permanent Slack message |
| bullets → narrative | `await draftStream.clear()` (chat.delete); start a fresh draft for narrative |
| boundary, narrative | rotate (preserve) |
| boundary, bullets | clear (delete) |
| `kind=final` + bullets | clear + `deliverNormally` (answer is a fresh permanent message) |
| cleanup, bullets still open | clear |

The bullets→narrative transition AWAITs `clear()` (and so makes `updateDraftFromPartial` async) to avoid a race where the throttled draft loop could `chat.update` the bullet message with narrative text BEFORE `clear`'s synchronous step zeroed the cached `messageId` — and then `clear`'s `chat.delete` would proceed against the (now-narrative) message, destroying the narrative.

`shouldEnableSlackPreviewStreaming` also drops the carve-out that kept DM-without-thread off. Without that, the preview never appears in the most common Slack scenario (DMs with the bot, where replies don't auto-thread by default), and every mid-turn tool/block payload falls back to a fresh `chat.postMessage` instead.

## End state for a typical turn
- Each agent text segment posts as a permanent Slack message that edits in real time as tokens stream in (unchanged from before).
- Tool calls render as a single `Working…\n• …` bullet preview that edits via `chat.update` as new tools fire (unchanged from before).
- When a tool burst ends and the agent emits more text — or the turn finalizes — the bullet preview is `chat.delete`'d. The channel ends the turn with only the agent's text content.

## Tests
- Updated `dispatch.streaming.test.ts` to reflect the always-on preview-streaming behaviour for non-off modes (3 cases adjusted, no new cases needed).
- **89 files / 894 tests pass**.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` clean.

## Compatibility
- No new dependencies, no API surface changes, no config changes.
- The `streaming.preview.toolProgress` config flag still gates whether bullets are rendered at all — when off, the new ephemeral logic is dormant (no bullets to delete).
- Apps registered as Slack 'Agents & AI Apps' using `chat.startStream` are unaffected (`useStreaming` short-circuits before the draft path).

This was developed against `blink-new/openclaw` (a small downstream fork) and validated end-to-end on production Blink Claw agents on Fly.io across multiple iterations.

target.title: fix(ios): stabilize chat streaming layout and session flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: iOS chat streaming could attach to a synthetic placeholder lane, churn during initial history/bootstrap, and reparse streaming content in ways that caused flicker or session drift.
- Why it matters: active conversations can show unstable streaming UI, lose the correct lane/session association, or visibly flicker while tokens arrive.
- What changed: scoped streaming to the active session key, hardened stream acceptance/initial load behavior, and stabilized layout parsing/visibility updates.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway protocol changes, no new model behavior. This fix lives in the shared `OpenClawChatUI` layer used by both iOS and macOS. The bug (cross-run UI drift during concurrent session-key agent events) affects any client using `ChatViewModel`; macOS uses the same chat view and benefits equally, but the fix is not platform-specific — it guards shared streaming state.
- AI-assisted: yes (AI-assisted implementation, contributor-reviewed).
- Testing level: targeted shared-package tests plus iOS build validation.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #40985
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: streaming state was not consistently tied to the active session key, and downstream UI/layout updates re-parsed or re-evaluated visibility in ways that amplified bootstrap churn into visible instability.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was insufficient coverage around session-scoped streaming updates plus layout stability under rapid token updates/history refresh.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): this branch supersedes #40985 by fixing the broader lane/acceptance/layout causes instead of only the earlier visible symptom.
- Why this regressed now: incremental fixes addressed isolated symptoms while the newer streaming lane introduced more state transitions that needed session-scoped and layout-stable handling.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: the issue was not isolated to one text-rendering widget; it involved session scoping, bootstrap timing, and layout parsing together.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/ChatViewModelTests.swift`
  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/AssistantTextParserTests.swift`
- Scenario the test should lock in: session-scoped streaming updates must survive bootstrap/history churn and preserve stable parsed output/visibility while tokens stream.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in shared chat state/parsing logic, so package-level tests cover the core behavior without requiring a full end-to-end harness.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): targeted parser/view-model tests were added/expanded for this branch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- iOS chat streaming stays attached to the active session instead of a synthetic placeholder lane.
- Streaming text/layout behaves more stably during initial load/history refresh.
- Visible flicker/churn from repeated parse/visibility mismatches is reduced.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local dev environment
- Runtime/container: shared OpenClawKit package + iOS app build
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): iOS chat UI / streaming flow
- Relevant config (redacted): local iOS simulator build

### Steps

1. Build the shared package and iOS app from this branch.
2. Open a chat session and trigger a streaming reply.
3. Observe initial history/bootstrap plus active token streaming.

### Expected

- Streaming binds to the active session.
- Bootstrap/history churn does not destabilize the active stream.
- Layout remains visually stable while tokens arrive.

### Actual

- Matched expected in targeted tests and local build validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter ChatViewModelTests`
  - `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter AssistantTextParserTests`
  - `xcodegen generate`
  - `xcodebuild -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClaw -destination 'generic/platform=iOS Simulator' CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO build`
- Edge cases checked:
  - stream acceptance during bootstrap/history load
  - parser stability under repeated streaming updates
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full repository CI matrix
  - non-iOS clients

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
  - All 19 review threads (P1/P2 from `chatgpt-codex-connector[bot]` + `copilot-pull-request-reviewer`) resolved in this update.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A



## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: chat-state timing bugs can reappear under bootstrapping or external-final sync edge cases.
  - Mitigation: this patch scopes state to the active session and adds targeted parser/view-model coverage around the churn paths.



## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: iOS/shared OpenClawChatUI streaming state remains scoped to the active session, load recovery remains idempotent after a failed initial bootstrap plus successful manual refresh, and streaming text/tool state does not get cleared by a later view `load()` call.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS local dev environment, Xcode 26.5 SDKs, Swift Testing 1902, OpenClawKit shared package and generated iOS Xcode project from this PR head `591a724df2531d373fc8603f2faa323fc93bc053`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter ChatViewModelTests
swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter AssistantTextParserTests
xcodegen generate --spec apps/ios/project.yml
xcodebuild -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClawChatUI -destination 'generic/platform=iOS Simulator' CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO build
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

